pd-merge-reshape.a: A many-to-many key (non-unique on both sides) multiplies rows. Catch it with validate='one_to_many' (or 'one_to_one') in merge, which raises if violated, and compare shapes before and after. It's the same fan-out trap as SQL joins.

pivot/pivot_table reshape long→wide (rows to columns; pivot_table also aggregates duplicates); melt reshapes wide→long (columns to rows — the tidy form). stack/unstack do the same via the Index. Use melt to tidy data, pivot to present it.

pd-timeseries-perf.q: Why avoid iterrows() and row-wise apply for performance?
pd-timeseries-perf.a: iterrows/itertuples and row-wise apply loop in Python with per-row overhead. Vectorized operations and built-in methods run in C over whole columns — often 10-100x faster. Use apply only when no vectorized form exists, and even then consider np.where or numpy.

pd-timeseries-perf.q: How do you work with time series in pandas?
pd-timeseries-perf.a: Use a DatetimeIndex (pd.to_datetime to parse), then resample('D'/'M') for downsampling/aggregation, rolling(window) for moving statistics, shift() for lags, and tz_localize/tz_convert for time zones. The DatetimeIndex unlocks time-aware grouping and alignment.

pd-timeseries-perf.q: When do you outgrow pandas, and what's next?
pd-timeseries-perf.a: When data approaches or exceeds RAM, or jobs are too slow — pandas is single-threaded and in-memory. Move to Polars (multi-threaded, lazy, larger-than-RAM streaming), DuckDB (SQL on files, out-of-core), or Spark/Dask (distributed). Often a small rewrite to Polars/DuckDB yields big wins.

pa-arrow.q: What is Apache Arrow and what problem does it solve?
pa-arrow.a: A language-agnostic, in-memory COLUMNAR format. It removes the (de)serialization overhead of moving data between tools/languages — anything that speaks Arrow shares data with zero copy, so pandas, Spark, DuckDB, and Polars exchange data instantly. It's the lingua franca of modern data tools.

pa-arrow.q: How does Arrow relate to Parquet?
pa-arrow.a: Arrow is the IN-MEMORY columnar format (optimized for compute); Parquet is the ON-DISK columnar format (optimized for storage and compression). They're complementary and designed together, so reading Parquet into Arrow memory is efficient. Arrow = runtime, Parquet = storage.

pa-arrow.q: What is zero-copy and why does it matter?
pa-arrow.a: Sharing data between components/languages by passing the same memory buffers instead of serializing and copying. Because Arrow defines a standard layout, a DuckDB result can become a pandas/Polars frame with no copy — eliminating the biggest hidden cost (serialization) in multi-tool pipelines.

pa-parquet.q: How do PyArrow datasets help with large Parquet data?
pa-parquet.a: pyarrow.dataset treats a directory of Parquet files (often partitioned, e.g. dt=.../) as one logical table with predicate pushdown and partition pruning — reading only the files, row-groups, and columns a query needs, lazily and out-of-core. You can scan terabytes without loading them.

pa-parquet.q: What is partition pruning and how do you enable it?
pa-parquet.a: Skipping whole partitions (subdirectories) that can't match a filter — WHERE dt='2024-01-01' reads only that dt=... folder. Enable it by writing Hive-style partitioned Parquet (partition columns) and filtering on the partition key; the engine prunes directories before reading any data.

pa-parquet.q: Why convert CSV to partitioned Parquet for a lake?
pa-parquet.a: Parquet is columnar (column pruning + compression + row-group stats for skipping) and typed; partitioning adds directory-level pruning. Together, queries scan a tiny fraction of the data versus CSV — often 10-100x faster and cheaper — which is why lakes store partitioned Parquet.

pl-dataframes.q: What is Polars and why is it faster than pandas?
pl-dataframes.a: A DataFrame library written in Rust on Arrow memory. It's multi-threaded (uses all cores), has a lazy query optimizer, is columnar and cache-efficient, and can stream larger-than-RAM data — whereas pandas is single-threaded and eager. The result is often 5-30x faster with lower memory.

pl-dataframes.q: How does Polars I/O differ from pandas?
pl-dataframes.a: Polars reads Parquet/CSV/JSON into Arrow-backed frames and, in lazy mode (scan_parquet), pushes projections and predicates down into the scan, reading only the needed columns and rows — and it can stream. pandas reads eagerly into memory. Use scan_* for large files.

pl-dataframes.q: When would you choose Polars over pandas?
pl-dataframes.a: For larger data, multi-core machines, or performance-critical transforms — Polars is faster, more memory-efficient, and handles bigger-than-RAM via streaming. pandas still wins on ecosystem maturity and tiny ad-hoc work. Many teams now use Polars for heavy ETL.

pl-expressions.q: What is the Polars expression API?
pl-expressions.a: A composable, declarative way to describe column computations — pl.col('x').filter(...).sum().over('group') — that Polars can parallelize and optimize. Expressions are the core idiom: you build them and apply via select/with_columns/group_by, rather than writing imperative row logic.

pl-expressions.q: How do Polars expressions enable parallelism?
pl-expressions.a: Because expressions are declarative (they describe WHAT, not HOW), the engine can evaluate independent column expressions across threads and reorder/optimize them. Writing transforms as expressions — not Python loops or apply — is exactly what unlocks Polars' multi-core speed.

pl-expressions.q: How does context (select vs with_columns vs group_by) change an expression?
pl-expressions.a: select returns only the chosen expressions; with_columns adds or overwrites columns while keeping the rest; group_by + agg evaluates expressions per group. The same expression behaves differently by context — pick the context for the output shape you want.

pl-lazy.q: Explain Polars lazy execution and its benefits.
pl-lazy.a: With lazy frames (scan_* / .lazy()), operations build a query PLAN that runs only on .collect(). The optimizer then applies projection pushdown (read only needed columns), predicate pushdown (filter at the scan), and reordering, and can stream larger-than-RAM. You get whole-query optimization like a database, instead of step-by-step eager work.

pl-lazy.q: Eager vs lazy in Polars — when each?
pl-lazy.a: Eager (DataFrame) executes immediately — good for interactive/small work. Lazy (LazyFrame) defers and optimizes the whole pipeline before running — use it for ETL and large data to get pushdown, fewer passes, and streaming. Build lazy, inspect with .explain(), then .collect().

pl-lazy.q: What is predicate and projection pushdown?
pl-lazy.a: The optimizer moves filters (predicate) and column selection (projection) down to the data scan, so only the needed rows and columns are read from Parquet/CSV in the first place — slashing I/O. It's the main reason lazy Polars and DuckDB are fast on big files.

duck-engine.q: What is DuckDB and when would you use it?
duck-engine.a: An in-process (embedded, like SQLite) analytical OLAP database — columnar, vectorized, no server. Use it to run fast SQL directly on Parquet/CSV/Arrow files locally, for analytics in a notebook or pipeline, or as a lightweight transform engine. Think 'SQLite for analytics.'

duck-engine.q: How can DuckDB query files larger than memory?
duck-engine.a: It's out-of-core and streaming: it reads Parquet column-by-column with projection and predicate pushdown, processes in vectorized batches, and spills to disk when needed — so SELECT ... FROM 'big/*.parquet' works without loading it all. It also parallelizes across cores.

duck-engine.q: DuckDB vs pandas vs Spark — where does it fit?
duck-engine.a: DuckDB sits between pandas (single-node, in-memory) and Spark (distributed cluster): fast multi-core SQL on a single machine over larger-than-RAM files with zero setup. Use it when pandas is too slow or too big but a Spark cluster is overkill — which covers a large share of real workloads.

duck-sql.q: How does DuckDB integrate with pandas/Arrow/Parquet?
duck-sql.a: It queries them directly: SELECT * FROM df (a pandas/Polars/Arrow object in scope) or FROM 'data/*.parquet' — zero-copy via Arrow, no import step — and returns Arrow/pandas/Polars. It's effectively a drop-in SQL engine over your existing dataframes and files.

duck-sql.q: Why run SQL on Parquet with DuckDB instead of loading it into pandas?
duck-sql.a: DuckDB reads only the needed columns and rows (pushdown), parallelizes, and works out-of-core — so it's faster and uses far less memory than pd.read_parquet of everything then filtering. You also get full analytical SQL (windows, joins) without standing up a warehouse.

duck-sql.q: Give a real data-engineering use for DuckDB.
duck-sql.a: Local transform and validation of lake files (SQL aggregations over partitioned Parquet), prototyping warehouse queries, powering a lightweight analytics API, or acting as the engine inside a Python ETL step — all without running a database server.

lib-dask.q: What is Dask and how does it scale pandas/NumPy?
lib-dask.a: Dask provides parallel, larger-than-memory versions of pandas/NumPy by partitioning data into chunks and building a lazy task graph executed across cores or a cluster. dask.dataframe mirrors the pandas API but processes partitions in parallel and out-of-core — scaling familiar code without a rewrite.

Dask is Python-native, integrates tightly with the PyData stack, and is great for scaling existing NumPy/pandas/scikit-learn work on a cluster or big single machine. Spark is JVM-based and more mature for very large distributed SQL/ETL. Choose Dask for Python-centric scaling, Spark for large-scale data platforms.

lib-dask.q: What's the catch with Dask's lazy task graphs?
lib-dask.a: Operations are lazy (build a graph) until .compute(), and cross-partition shuffles (groupby/merge) can be expensive, while poor partition sizing hurts performance. You must think about partitions and avoid triggering huge shuffles — similar discipline to Spark.

sa-core.q: What is SQLAlchemy Core and the Engine?
sa-core.a: Core is SQLAlchemy's SQL toolkit (below the ORM): the Engine manages connectivity and a connection POOL, and you build database-agnostic, parameterized SQL with the expression language or text(). It's ideal for ETL where you want SQL control and pooling without ORM overhead.

Opening a DB connection is expensive (TCP + auth). A pool reuses a set of open connections across queries, cutting latency and DB load. The Engine pools by default (tune pool_size/max_overflow). Without pooling, a busy app exhausts the database and pays connection cost on every query.

sa-core.q: Core vs ORM — when use which?
sa-core.a: Use Core for ETL and bulk SQL where you want explicit, efficient, set-based statements (inserts, upserts, analytical queries). Use the ORM for application domains with objects, relationships, and unit-of-work persistence. Many data pipelines prefer Core or raw SQL for performance and clarity.

sa-orm.q: What does the ORM give you, and what is the session?
sa-orm.a: It maps Python classes to tables (models) and rows to objects, so you work with objects instead of SQL. The Session is the unit-of-work: it tracks object changes and flushes them as SQL on commit, managing identity and transactions. Great for app domains, heavier for bulk DE.

sa-orm.q: What is the N+1 query problem and how do you fix it?
sa-orm.a: Lazily loading a relationship inside a loop fires one query per parent (1 + N total). Fix it with eager loading — joinedload (a JOIN) or selectinload (a second IN query) — to fetch related rows in one or two queries. It's the classic ORM performance trap.

sa-orm.q: Why might you avoid the ORM for heavy data loads?
sa-orm.a: The ORM's per-object change tracking and instantiation add overhead that's wasteful for bulk operations. To load millions of rows, use Core/bulk inserts, executemany, or COPY — set-based SQL — rather than creating and persisting individual objects. Right tool for the job.

lib-pydantic.q: What does Pydantic do and where do you use it?
lib-pydantic.a: It validates and parses data against typed models — coercing and checking types, applying constraints, and raising clear errors on bad input. Use it at trust boundaries: API request/response bodies, config/settings, and validating ingested records before they enter the pipeline. It turns 'hope the data is right' into enforced schemas.

dataclasses are stdlib, lightweight, and do NOT validate — good for internal records. Pydantic validates and coerces at runtime (and parses from dict/JSON) with rich constraints and precise error messages — good for untrusted/external data. Dataclass inside the system, Pydantic at the edges.

lib-pydantic.q: How does Pydantic improve pipeline reliability?
lib-pydantic.a: By validating each record against a schema at ingestion, it fails fast on malformed, missing, or wrong-typed fields with precise errors — so bad data is caught at the boundary and quarantined rather than corrupting downstream tables. It's data-contract enforcement in code.

lib-requests.q: How do you make a robust HTTP GET in Python?
lib-requests.a: requests.get(url, params=..., headers=..., timeout=10); then resp.raise_for_status() and resp.json(). Always set a timeout (or it can hang forever), check the status, and handle exceptions. Use a Session to reuse the connection across many calls.

lib-requests.q: requests vs httpx — why choose httpx?
lib-requests.a: httpx has a requests-like API plus async support (await client.get), HTTP/2, and connection pooling via a client. Choose httpx for high-concurrency async ingestion (many APIs at once); requests for simple synchronous calls. Both support sessions/clients for connection reuse.

lib-requests.q: Why use a Session/Client for repeated calls?
lib-requests.a: It reuses the underlying TCP connection (pooling), persists headers/auth/cookies, and is much faster than a fresh connection per request. For paginated or bulk API ingestion, a session avoids repeated handshakes and lets you set defaults once.

lib-great-expectations.q: What is Great Expectations and what does it solve?
lib-great-expectations.a: A data-quality framework: you declare 'expectations' (assertions like column-not-null, values-in-set, row-count-in-range) and validate datasets against them, producing pass/fail results and data docs. It turns data quality into testable, versioned, automated checks in your pipeline.

lib-great-expectations.q: Where in a pipeline do data-quality checks belong?
lib-great-expectations.a: At boundaries — validate raw data right after ingestion (catch source problems early) and validate modeled tables before serving. On failure, quarantine/alert or circuit-break critical tables. GE (or dbt tests/Soda) runs these in CI and at runtime, tracked over time.

lib-great-expectations.q: GE vs dbt tests vs Pydantic?
lib-great-expectations.a: Pydantic validates individual records (row-level, at ingestion, in Python). dbt tests assert SQL-level rules on warehouse tables (not-null, unique, relationships). Great Expectations validates datasets (files/frames/tables) with a rich expectation library plus docs. They overlap; choose by where the data lives and the granularity you need.

lib-boto3.q: What is boto3 and how do you use it for S3?
lib-boto3.a: The AWS SDK for Python. Create a client (boto3.client('s3')) and call operations (put_object, get_object, list_objects_v2, upload_file). Credentials resolve from env vars, then a shared profile, then an IAM role. It covers S3 plus DynamoDB, SQS, and the rest of AWS.

lib-boto3.q: How should boto3 credentials be managed in production?
lib-boto3.a: Use IAM roles (EC2/ECS/Lambda execution roles) so credentials are provided and rotated automatically — never hard-code access keys or commit them. boto3's default chain resolves env, then profile, then role. Grant least-privilege IAM policies scoped to the needed buckets and actions.

lib-boto3.q: How do you handle large S3 listings and downloads efficiently?
lib-boto3.a: Use paginators (get_paginator('list_objects_v2')) to iterate beyond the 1000-key page limit; use managed multipart transfers (upload_file/download_file with TransferConfig) for big objects; and filter by prefix. For data access, fsspec/s3fs often gives a simpler file-like API.

lib-fsspec.a: A unified, file-like API over many backends — local, S3 (s3fs), GCS (gcsfs), Azure, HTTP — so the same code (fsspec.open('s3://...')) works across storage. pandas/Polars/PyArrow/Dask use it under the hood, which is why pd.read_parquet('s3://...') just works. It lets you write storage-agnostic pipelines.

lib-fsspec.q: Why is fsspec useful for portable pipelines?
lib-fsspec.a: You write to a path or URL (s3://, gs://, file://) and fsspec selects the right filesystem, so the same ingestion/transform code runs locally in dev and against cloud storage in prod with only a config change — no backend-specific code scattered around.

lib-fsspec.q: How do pandas/Polars read directly from S3?
lib-fsspec.a: Via fsspec/s3fs: pd.read_parquet('s3://bucket/key.parquet', storage_options={...}). fsspec resolves the s3:// URL, handles auth, and streams the bytes — so cloud files behave like local ones to the dataframe library.

lib-prefect.q: What is Prefect and what does an orchestrator provide?
lib-prefect.a: A Python-native workflow orchestrator. You decorate functions as @task and compose them in a @flow; Prefect handles scheduling, retries, dependencies, state, logging, and an observability UI. Orchestrators turn a script into a reliable, monitored, schedulable pipeline (like Airflow or Dagster).

Airflow defines DAGs statically and is the mature, ubiquitous standard. Prefect is more Python-native and dynamic (flows are just code, dynamic mapping, easier local dev) with a modern API. Both schedule, retry, and monitor; choose by ecosystem fit and how dynamic your workflows are.

lib-prefect.q: Why use an orchestrator instead of cron plus scripts?
lib-prefect.a: Cron runs scripts but gives no dependency management, retries, backfills, state, logging, or visibility. An orchestrator adds retries on failure, dependency ordering, parameterized/backfilled runs, alerting, and a UI to see what ran and why it failed — essential for reliable production pipelines.

lib-beam.q: What is Apache Beam's core idea?
lib-beam.a: A unified programming model for BOTH batch and streaming: you write one pipeline (PCollections plus transforms like ParDo/GroupByKey/windowing) and run it on different engines (runners) — Dataflow, Flink, Spark. 'Write once, run anywhere' for data processing, with first-class event-time windowing.

lib-beam.q: How does Beam handle late and out-of-order streaming data?
lib-beam.a: Through event-time windowing plus watermarks and triggers: it groups events by event time into windows, the watermark estimates progress, and triggers control when (and how often) results emit, with allowed lateness for stragglers. That's the unified model's answer to streaming correctness.

lib-beam.q: Batch vs streaming in Beam — what changes?
lib-beam.a: Conceptually little — the same transforms apply; you switch the source (bounded file vs unbounded stream) and add windowing/triggers for streaming. That unification (one API for both) is Beam's main selling point, so you don't maintain two separate codebases.

lib-faker.q: What is Faker and a DE use for it?
lib-faker.a: A library that generates realistic fake data — names, emails, addresses, dates, companies. DE uses: seed dev/test databases, generate sample data for pipeline tests and demos, and load-test systems — without touching real PII. fake = Faker(); fake.name(), fake.email().

lib-faker.q: How do you make Faker output reproducible?
lib-faker.a: Seed it: Faker.seed(42) (and seed_instance for an instance) so the same sequence is generated each run — essential for deterministic tests. Use fake.unique.x() for unique values (keys) and locales (Faker('ja_JP')) for region-specific data.

lib-faker.q: Why generate synthetic data instead of using production data?
lib-faker.a: To avoid exposing real PII in dev/test/CI (privacy and compliance), to create data at any volume for load testing, and to cover edge cases that may not exist in production. Synthetic data lets you build and test safely without copying sensitive records.

lib-openpyxl.q: How do you read and write Excel files in Python?
lib-openpyxl.a: pandas (read_excel/to_excel, backed by openpyxl) for tabular data. For fine control — formatting, formulas, multiple sheets, charts — use openpyxl directly, or XlsxWriter for fast write-only with rich formatting. Excel remains a common business interchange format.

lib-openpyxl.q: openpyxl vs XlsxWriter vs pandas — when each?
lib-openpyxl.a: pandas for quick dataframe-to-sheet I/O; openpyxl to read AND edit existing workbooks (preserve or modify cells and styles); XlsxWriter for high-performance writing of new files with advanced formatting and charts (write-only). Pick by read-vs-write and formatting needs.

lib-openpyxl.q: Why is Excel risky as a data source, and how do you handle it?
lib-openpyxl.a: Excel mixes data with formatting, has type ambiguity (dates/numbers stored as text), merged cells, and manual edits — fragile for pipelines. Handle it defensively: explicit dtypes, skiprows, validate headers, validate after load, and convert to a clean typed format (Parquet) early.

lib-matplotlib.q: What is matplotlib and the Figure/Axes model?
lib-matplotlib.a: Python's foundational plotting library. A Figure is the canvas; Axes are the individual plots on it (fig, ax = plt.subplots()). You draw on the Axes (ax.plot, ax.bar) and customize labels and limits. Most other libraries (pandas .plot, seaborn) sit on top of matplotlib.

plt.plot() uses an implicit 'current' figure/axes — quick but ambiguous with multiple plots. The OO API (fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.plot(...)) is explicit and preferred for anything beyond a quick chart — clearer, composable, and required for subplots.

lib-matplotlib.q: matplotlib vs seaborn vs plotly — when each?
lib-matplotlib.a: matplotlib for full control and as the base layer; seaborn for quick statistical plots from dataframes with good defaults (built on matplotlib); plotly for interactive web charts. For static EDA reach for seaborn; for fine-tuned/publication figures, matplotlib; for dashboards, plotly.

lib-seaborn.q: What is seaborn and how does it relate to matplotlib?
lib-seaborn.a: A statistical visualization library built ON matplotlib that produces attractive plots from pandas DataFrames with little code — histplot, boxplot, scatterplot, heatmap. It handles aggregation and grouping (hue/col) with good defaults, and you can still drop to matplotlib to customize.

lib-seaborn.q: What's the value of hue/col/row in seaborn?
lib-seaborn.a: They split a plot by a categorical column into colored series (hue) or small-multiple grids (col/row), so you compare groups without manual loops. It's the fast path to faceted, grouped EDA straight from a tidy DataFrame.

lib-seaborn.q: Which plots do you reach for in EDA?
lib-seaborn.a: Distributions: histplot/kdeplot/boxplot; relationships: scatterplot/lineplot (+ regression via lmplot); categories: barplot/countplot; correlations: a heatmap of df.corr(); and pairplot for an overview. Seaborn makes these one-liners from a DataFrame.

unix-intro.q: Why do data engineers need the command line?
unix-intro.a: Data lives on servers and clusters you reach over SSH and the shell; you inspect and move files, run jobs, tail logs, and pipe tools together — often faster than writing code. The CLI is the universal interface to data infrastructure.

unix-intro.q: Explain the Unix filesystem and basic navigation.
unix-intro.a: A single tree rooted at /, where everything is a path. pwd shows where you are, ls lists, cd moves; absolute paths start at / and relative paths (../data) start from the current directory. The tree plus navigation is the baseline shell skill.
Small programs that each do one thing well, operate on text streams, and compose via pipes. That's why grep/sort/awk combine so powerfully — and it's a good model for designing pipeline steps too.

unix-pipes.q: Explain pipes and the three standard streams.
unix-pipes.a: Every process has stdin (0), stdout (1), and stderr (2). A pipe | connects one command's stdout to the next's stdin, streaming data through a chain with no temp files. stderr stays separate so error messages don't pollute the data stream.
> redirects stdout to a file (overwrite); >> appends; 2> redirects stderr; &> or 2>&1 combines both. Redirection sends streams to files, while pipes send them to other commands.

unix-pipes.q: How do pipes process a file bigger than RAM?
unix-pipes.a: Each stage streams line by line, so memory stays flat regardless of file size — cat huge.csv | grep x | sort processes incrementally. It's the same lazy/streaming principle as Python generators.

unix-text.q: When do you reach for grep vs sed vs awk?
unix-text.a: grep filters lines matching a pattern; sed edits/substitutes text (s/old/new/); awk processes columns and fields with logic ('{print $1,$3}', sums, conditions). grep = find, sed = edit, awk = columnar compute.

unix-text.q: Sum the 3rd column of a CSV with awk.
unix-text.a: awk -F, '{sum += $3} END {print sum}' file.csv. -F, sets the field separator, $3 is the third field, and END runs after all lines. awk is a tiny data-processing language ideal for quick column math.

unix-text.q: Count how many log lines contain 'ERROR'.
unix-text.a: grep -c ERROR app.log (or grep ERROR app.log | wc -l). grep -c counts matching lines directly; for distinct counts, pipe to sort | uniq -c.

unix-permissions.q: Explain Unix file permissions and chmod 755.
unix-permissions.a: Each file has rwx (read/write/execute) for user, group, and other. In octal r=4, w=2, x=1, summed per group — 755 means rwx (7) for the user and r-x (5) for group and other. chmod sets permissions, chown changes ownership; on a directory, x is permission to enter it.

unix-permissions.q: How do you find and kill a runaway process?
unix-permissions.a: Find it with ps aux | grep name or top to get the PID, then kill PID (or kill -9 to force). Run jobs in the background with & and manage them with jobs/fg/bg; nohup/tmux keep them alive after logout.

unix-permissions.q: What does sudo do and why be careful?
unix-permissions.a: sudo runs a command as the superuser (root), bypassing permission restrictions. It's powerful but dangerous — a wrong sudo command (like rm -rf) has no guardrails. Apply least privilege and avoid running pipelines as root.

unix-scripting.q: What goes at the top of a bash script, and why set -euo pipefail?
unix-scripting.a: A shebang (#!/usr/bin/env bash) selects the interpreter. set -e exits on any error, -u errors on unset variables, and -o pipefail fails a pipeline if any stage fails — together they make scripts fail fast and safe instead of silently continuing after an error.

unix-scripting.q: How do variables and arguments work in bash?
unix-scripting.a: var=value (no spaces), referenced as "$var" — always quote, since filenames contain spaces. Script arguments are $1, $2, ..., with $@ for all and $# for the count; command substitution is result=$(cmd). Quoting is the number-one source of bash bugs.

unix-scripting.q: When should you switch from bash to Python?
unix-scripting.a: Bash is great for gluing commands, simple loops, and file plumbing. Switch to Python when you need real data structures, complex logic, robust error handling, JSON/API work, or anything beyond roughly 50 lines — bash gets unreadable and fragile fast. Use each for its strength.

unix-loops.q: Loop over every CSV in a folder.
unix-loops.a: for f in data/*.csv; do echo "processing $f"; done — loop over a GLOB and quote "$f". Use shopt -s nullglob so an empty match skips the loop instead of looping on the literal pattern. Never loop over $(ls), which breaks on spaces.

unix-loops.q: Read a file line by line safely in bash.
unix-loops.a: while IFS= read -r line; do ...; done < file. IFS= preserves leading/trailing whitespace, -r stops backslash interpretation, and < feeds the file. Beware: cmd | while ... runs in a subshell (variables set inside are lost) — use done < <(cmd) instead.

unix-loops.q: Retry a command up to 5 times with backoff.
unix-loops.a: until cmd; do attempt=$((attempt+1)); [ $attempt -ge 5 ] && exit 1; sleep $((2**attempt)); done. until loops until success, and exponential backoff (2,4,8,...) spaces the retries — the resilient-ingestion pattern in shell.

unix-automation.q: Explain cron and its five fields.
unix-automation.a: cron schedules recurring jobs. The five fields are minute, hour, day-of-month, month, and day-of-week, followed by the command — e.g. 0 6 * * * runs daily at 06:00. crontab -e edits jobs. It's the simplest scheduler for periodic tasks.

unix-automation.q: What are cron's limits, and what do you use at scale?
unix-automation.a: Cron has no dependency management, retries, backfills, logging, or visibility, and it's per-machine. For real pipelines use an orchestrator (Airflow/Dagster/Prefect) that adds DAG dependencies, retries, alerting, and a UI. Cron is fine for a single simple job.

unix-automation.q: How do you capture a cron job's output and errors?
unix-automation.a: Redirect in the crontab: ... >> /var/log/job.log 2>&1 captures stdout and stderr, since cron jobs run without a terminal. Cron emails output by default, but logging to a file is more reliable — always log, because debugging a silent cron failure is painful.

unix-de.q: How do you inspect a 50 GB CSV you can't open in an editor?
unix-de.a: head -100 for the first rows, tail -f to follow, wc -l to count rows, less to page through, awk/cut to peek at columns, and grep to find rows — all stream without loading the file. The CLI inspects huge files in seconds before any Python touches them.

unix-de.q: Count distinct values of a column in a big CSV from the shell.
unix-de.a: cut -d, -f3 data.csv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn — cut extracts the column, sort plus uniq -c counts occurrences, and sort -rn ranks them. It's a complete group-by-count in one streaming pipe, no database needed.

unix-de.q: How do you parallelize a per-file CLI task?
unix-de.a: Use xargs -P (or GNU parallel): ls *.csv | xargs -P 4 -I {} ./process.sh {} runs 4 at a time. It's often simpler and faster than a backgrounded (&) loop for embarrassingly parallel file processing, and it bounds concurrency.

model-normalization.a: Normalize (3NF) for OLTP — integrity, cheap writes, no anomalies. Denormalize for analytics/warehouses — precompute joins into wide star-schema tables so big scans avoid expensive runtime joins, accepting redundancy. The workload decides: many small writes → normalize; few big reads → denormalize.

model-normalization.q: Why is a normalized model bad for analytics?
model-normalization.a: Normalized data is spread across many tables, so analytical queries need many joins over huge tables — slow. Analytics favors fewer, wider denormalized tables (star schema) so scans avoid joins. Integrity, the point of normalization, matters less for a read-only analytical copy that's loaded in bulk.

model-normalization.q: What is an update anomaly, and why is it acceptable in a warehouse?
model-normalization.a: It's when redundant data lets one copy update while others don't, creating inconsistency (a customer's city on every order row). 3NF removes redundancy so each fact lives once. In a warehouse you accept controlled redundancy because data is loaded/refreshed in bulk, not updated row-by-row, so the anomaly is managed by reloads.

model-star-schema.a: A central fact table (measures + foreign keys) surrounded by dimension tables holding descriptive context (who/what/when/where). It's denormalized for fast analytical queries — few joins, big scans — and is the workhorse of dimensional/Kimball modeling.

Star keeps each dimension denormalized in one table — fewer joins, faster, simpler. Snowflake normalizes dimensions into sub-tables (product → category → department) — less redundancy but more joins and slower. Star is the analytics default; snowflake only when dimensions are very large or shared.

model-star-schema.q: What goes in a fact vs a dimension?
model-star-schema.a: Facts hold measures (numbers you aggregate: amount, qty) plus foreign keys, at a declared grain. Dimensions hold descriptive attributes (names, categories, dates) you filter and group by. Rule of thumb: if you SUM it, it's a measure; if you filter/group by it, it's a dimension attribute.

model-scd2.a: Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 preserves history: when an attribute changes you INSERT a new row (new surrogate key) with valid_from/valid_to dates and an is_current flag, keeping the old row. Facts join to the version that was current at the event time, so historical reports reflect the past state, not today's.

model-scd2.q: SCD Type 1 vs Type 2 vs Type 3?
model-scd2.a: Type 1 OVERWRITES the old value (no history, simplest). Type 2 adds a new row to KEEP history (valid_from/to, is_current). Type 3 keeps limited history in extra columns (previous_value). Use Type 2 when you need point-in-time accuracy — e.g., which region a customer was in when they ordered.

model-scd2.q: How do facts join correctly to an SCD2 dimension?
model-scd2.a: The fact stores the surrogate key of the dimension VERSION current at the event time, resolved during load via a point-in-time lookup on valid_from/valid_to — not the natural key. So a 2022 order joins to the 2022 version of the customer. This is exactly why surrogate keys plus effective dates exist.

model-warehouse-vs-lake.a: Warehouse = schema-on-write, structured, fast governed SQL (rigid, costly for raw). Lake = schema-on-read, any raw data, cheap and flexible (risks a swamp). Lakehouse = a table layer (Delta/Iceberg) on the lake giving ACID + schema + SQL on cheap storage — the modern default combining the lake's cost with the warehouse's reliability.

model-warehouse-vs-lake.q: When choose a lake over a warehouse?
model-warehouse-vs-lake.a: For cheap storage of large, raw, semi-, or unstructured data, ML workloads, and schema-on-read flexibility. Warehouses win for fast, governed SQL on modeled structured data. Increasingly the lakehouse blends both — store once on object storage, query with warehouse-like reliability.

model-warehouse-vs-lake.q: What is a data swamp and how do you avoid it?
model-warehouse-vs-lake.a: A lake with no governance, catalog, or quality — undiscoverable, untrusted, unusable data. Avoid it with a catalog/metadata, schema enforcement (table formats), quality checks, ownership, and a medallion (bronze/silver/gold) structure. The lakehouse table layer is partly a response to swamps.

model-fact-dimension.q: What is the grain of a fact table, and why declare it first?
model-fact-dimension.a: The grain is what one row represents (one order line item, one daily balance). Declaring it first is the cornerstone of dimensional design — it fixes which dimensions and measures are valid and prevents mixing detail levels (double counting). Always state the grain before adding any column.

model-fact-dimension.q: Additive vs semi-additive vs non-additive measures?
model-fact-dimension.a: Additive sums across all dimensions (revenue). Semi-additive sums across some but not time (an account balance — you snapshot it, you don't sum balances across days). Non-additive can't be summed (ratios, percentages — recompute from components). Knowing which prevents wrong aggregations.

model-fact-dimension.q: What is a factless fact table?
model-fact-dimension.a: A fact table with only foreign keys and no measures — it records that an event or relationship happened (a student attended a class, a promotion covered a product). You query it by counting rows or checking coverage (e.g., which products had no promotion).

model-keys.q: Surrogate vs natural key — which for a dimension PK and why?
model-keys.a: Use a surrogate key (a meaningless integer) as the dimension primary key: it's stable (natural keys like email change), compact for joins, decouples the warehouse from source systems, and is required for SCD2 (each version gets its own surrogate). Keep the natural/business key as an attribute for lookups.

model-keys.q: Why not use the source system's primary key directly?
model-keys.a: Source keys can change, collide across systems (two sources both have id=1), be reused, or expose PII, and they tie your model to source structure. A surrogate key insulates the warehouse, lets you integrate multiple sources, and supports historical versioning (SCD2).

A dimension key that lives in the fact table with no associated dimension table — like order_number or invoice_number. It's useful for grouping and drill-down (all lines of an order) but has no descriptive attributes of its own, so it stays on the fact.

model-denormalization.a: To eliminate expensive runtime joins on huge scans — precompute the joins into wide tables (star schema, OBT) so reads are fast. You trade storage and update complexity (redundancy) for query speed. It's valid because analytical data is loaded in bulk and read-heavy, so update anomalies are less of a concern.

model-denormalization.q: What are the costs of denormalization?
model-denormalization.a: Redundancy (more storage), update complexity (a change must propagate to all copies — handled by full reloads in warehouses), and risk of inconsistency if unmanaged. You accept these for read performance. The discipline: denormalize deliberately in the serving layer while keeping normalized/atomic data upstream.

model-denormalization.q: When is denormalization the wrong choice?
model-denormalization.a: In OLTP/transactional systems where data changes frequently and integrity matters — redundancy causes update anomalies and bloated writes. Also when the join is already cheap or the wide table becomes unwieldy. Normalize for write-heavy operational systems; denormalize for read-heavy analytics.

model-inmon-kimball.a: Inmon (top-down): build a normalized 3NF enterprise data warehouse as the single source of truth first, then derive dimensional marts. Kimball (bottom-up): build dimensional star-schema marts directly, integrated via conformed dimensions. Inmon emphasizes integration/governance first; Kimball emphasizes faster delivery and analytics-friendly models.

model-inmon-kimball.q: What are conformed dimensions and why do they matter?
model-inmon-kimball.a: Dimensions shared and consistent across multiple fact tables and marts (the same dim_date, dim_customer everywhere). They let you compare and combine across business processes (sales and returns by the same customer/date) — the integration glue of Kimball's bus architecture.

model-inmon-kimball.q: Which approach do modern stacks tend to use?
model-inmon-kimball.a: Often a pragmatic hybrid: land raw, model Kimball-style dimensional stars in the serving layer (dbt), while keeping integrated atomic data upstream. Pure Inmon EDWs are heavier; Kimball's dimensional marts dominate analytics, and the lakehouse + dbt era favors fast, conformed dimensional models.

model-data-vault.q: What is Data Vault and when use it?
model-data-vault.a: A modeling approach for the integration layer built from Hubs (business keys), Links (relationships), and Satellites (descriptive attributes + history). It's optimized for auditability, scalability, and absorbing change from many sources — you add satellites/links without restructuring. Use it for large, evolving, audit-heavy enterprise warehouses, then build Kimball marts on top for consumption.

model-data-vault.q: Hubs, Links, and Satellites — what is each?
model-data-vault.a: A Hub is a unique business key (customer_id) plus metadata. A Link is a relationship between hubs (customer-order). A Satellite holds descriptive, time-stamped attributes hanging off a hub or link, and is where history lives. The separation makes the model resilient to change and fully auditable.

Data Vault is for the raw/integration layer — flexible, auditable, source-agnostic, but not query-friendly. Kimball star schemas are for the consumption layer — denormalized, fast, analyst-friendly. They're complementary: Vault to integrate and historize, then dimensional marts on top for BI.

model-advanced-dims.a: One physical dimension referenced multiple times in a fact under different roles — e.g., a single dim_date joined as order_date, ship_date, and delivery_date, exposed via views or aliases per role. It avoids duplicating the date dimension while serving multiple date columns.

A single dimension that bundles several low-cardinality flags/indicators (is_returned, payment_type, channel) that would otherwise clutter the fact with many columns or spawn many tiny dimensions. It stores the distinct combinations, keeping the fact narrow and the model clean.

A table that resolves a many-to-many relationship between a fact and a dimension (or two dimensions) — e.g., one bank account with multiple customers. It sits between them holding the keys, often with a weighting factor to avoid double-counting. It's how dimensional models handle M:N without breaking the star.

model-semantic-layer.q: What is a semantic/metrics layer and why does it matter?
model-semantic-layer.a: A centralized layer that defines business metrics (revenue, active users) and their logic ONCE, so every tool and dashboard computes them consistently. It decouples metric definitions from individual reports, ending the 'why do two dashboards show different revenue?' problem. Tools: dbt Semantic Layer, Cube, LookML.

model-semantic-layer.q: What problem does a metrics layer solve?
model-semantic-layer.a: Metric drift — different teams re-implement 'revenue' or 'churn' slightly differently across BI tools, producing conflicting numbers and eroding trust. A semantic layer defines each metric and its dimensions centrally, governed and version-controlled, so everyone queries the same definition.

model-semantic-layer.q: Where does the semantic layer sit?
model-semantic-layer.a: Between the modeled warehouse tables and the consumption tools (BI, notebooks, APIs). It translates business metric requests into governed SQL over the underlying tables — it's the contract between the data team's tables and the business's metrics.

model-snowflake.q: What is a snowflake schema and its trade-off vs star?
model-snowflake.a: A snowflake schema NORMALIZES dimensions into related sub-tables (product → category → department) instead of one flat dimension. It saves storage and reduces redundancy but adds joins, making queries slower and more complex. Star (denormalized) is usually preferred for analytics; snowflake when dimensions are very large or shared.

model-snowflake.q: When would you normalize a dimension?
model-snowflake.a: When a dimension is very large with repeated sub-attributes (saving significant space), when a sub-hierarchy is shared across dimensions, or when source data is naturally hierarchical. Otherwise keep dimensions flat (star) for query simplicity and speed — joins are the cost.

model-snowflake.q: Star vs snowflake in one line?
model-snowflake.a: Star = denormalized dimensions (fewer joins, faster, simpler); snowflake = normalized dimensions (more joins, less redundancy). Analytics defaults to star.

model-obt.q: What is One Big Table (OBT), and when use it?
model-obt.a: A single wide, fully-denormalized table with facts plus all dimension attributes flattened in — no joins at query time. Modern columnar engines (BigQuery, Snowflake, DuckDB) handle wide tables efficiently, so OBT can be faster and simpler for analysts than a star. Use it in the serving layer where query simplicity and speed matter.

OBT: zero joins, simplest for analysts, fast on columnar engines — but redundant, larger, and harder to keep consistent (a dimension change must update every row). Star: less redundancy, easier SCD and maintenance, but requires joins. Columnar warehouses have pushed many teams toward OBT/wide tables for the serving layer.

model-obt.q: Why are wide tables viable now when they weren't before?
model-obt.a: Columnar storage plus cheap compute. Columnar formats read only the columns a query needs (column pruning) and compress repeated dimension values well, so a 200-column OBT isn't penalized like in a row store. Storage is cheap and joins are the expensive part — so you trade cheap storage for avoided joins.

model-aggregates.q: What are aggregate/summary tables and why use them?
model-aggregates.a: Pre-computed rollups of a detailed fact (e.g., daily or monthly totals per store) stored as smaller tables. Common dashboard queries hit the small aggregate in milliseconds instead of scanning billions of detail rows. You trade storage and refresh cost for big read speedups, keeping the detailed fact for drill-down.

model-aggregates.q: How do you keep aggregates consistent with the detail, and what's the risk?
model-aggregates.a: Rebuild or incrementally refresh them from the detail on a schedule, ideally in the same pipeline so they can't drift. The risks are staleness (the aggregate lags the detail) and double-maintenance. Some engines auto-route queries to aggregates (aggregate awareness); otherwise the semantic/BI layer or the query must target them.

Same idea — precomputed results for speed. A materialized view is the database-managed form (the engine refreshes it and may auto-rewrite queries to use it); an aggregate table is an explicitly built-and-scheduled summary you manage in your pipeline (more control, more responsibility). Both trade freshness/refresh cost for read speed.

nosql-types.q: What are the four NoSQL families?
nosql-types.a: Key-value (Redis, DynamoDB — fast lookups by key), Document (MongoDB — flexible JSON-like docs), Wide-column (Cassandra, HBase — dynamic columns, write-heavy at scale), and Graph (Neo4j — nodes + relationships for connected data). Each fits a different access pattern.

nosql-types.q: When choose NoSQL over a relational DB?
nosql-types.a: When you need horizontal scale beyond one node, a flexible/evolving schema, very high write throughput, or a specific access pattern (key lookups, graph traversal) that relational handles poorly. Relational still wins for complex joins, transactions, and ad-hoc queries. Choose by access pattern and scale, not hype.

nosql-types.q: What does NoSQL actually trade away?
nosql-types.a: Usually rich querying (joins, ad-hoc SQL) and strong ACID transactions, in exchange for horizontal scalability, flexible schema, and performance on a specific access pattern. Many are eventually consistent. You model around known queries rather than getting general-purpose querying for free.

nosql-cap.a: During a network partition, a distributed system must choose Consistency (reject reads/writes it can't confirm) or Availability (answer anyway, possibly stale) — not both. With no partition you can have both. CP systems prioritize correctness (HBase); AP prioritize uptime (Cassandra, Dynamo). It frames the fundamental trade-off.

nosql-cap.q: Give a CP vs AP example and why.
nosql-cap.a: A banking ledger leans CP — better an error than a wrong balance during a partition. A shopping cart or social feed leans AP — better to stay available and reconcile later than go down. The choice follows whether stale data or unavailability is worse; many systems make it tunable.

nosql-cap.q: What is PACELC and why is it better than CAP?
nosql-cap.a: PACELC extends CAP: if Partition, trade Availability vs Consistency (PAC); Else (normal operation), trade Latency vs Consistency (ELC). It captures that even without partitions you trade consistency for latency (e.g., reading a replica) — a more complete description of real distributed databases.

nosql-document-kv.a: For simple, fast lookups by a known key — sessions, caches, user profiles, feature flags, shopping carts. O(1) get/put at massive scale, with no querying by value. If your access is 'give me the value for this key', KV is the simplest and fastest fit (Redis, DynamoDB).

Document stores (MongoDB) hold flexible JSON-like documents — good when the schema varies/evolves, data is hierarchical, and you read whole documents by key (denormalized aggregates). Relational wins for normalized data with complex joins and strong transactions. Document = schema flexibility + aggregate-oriented reads.

nosql-document-kv.q: What's a downside of document stores?
nosql-document-kv.a: Querying across documents and joins are weak or expensive, and denormalization means an update must touch many documents (or you accept duplication). Without discipline, flexible schema becomes inconsistent schema. You model around access patterns up front rather than querying freely later.

nosql-wide-column-graph.q: How does Cassandra achieve high write throughput and scale?
nosql-wide-column-graph.a: It's a wide-column store with a masterless (peer-to-peer) ring, consistent-hashing partitioning, and LSM-tree storage — writes are fast appends to a memtable + commit log, flushed to immutable SSTables. It's AP with tunable consistency, scales linearly by adding nodes, and is optimized for write-heavy known-query workloads (you model a table per query).

nosql-wide-column-graph.q: When do you reach for a graph database?
nosql-wide-column-graph.a: When relationships are first-class and you traverse them — social networks, fraud rings, recommendations, knowledge graphs. Graph DBs (Neo4j) make multi-hop traversals (friends-of-friends, shortest path) fast and expressive, which would be costly recursive joins in SQL. If your queries are about connections, use a graph.

nosql-wide-column-graph.q: Why is data modeling 'query-first' in wide-column stores?
nosql-wide-column-graph.a: Because there are no ad-hoc joins and queries must hit the partition key efficiently, you design tables AROUND the exact queries you'll run (one table per access pattern), often duplicating data. Unlike relational (model the data, then query), you model the queries first — and getting the partition key right is everything.

nosql-sharding-replication.q: Explain sharding and the danger of a bad shard key.
nosql-sharding-replication.a: Sharding splits data across nodes by a key so each holds a subset → horizontal scale and parallel throughput. A bad key creates hot spots (skew) — sharding by date sends all of today's writes to one node; low cardinality clusters data. A good key is high-cardinality and evenly distributed (often a hash) so load spreads evenly.

Leader-follower (primary-replica): writes go to the leader and replicate to followers (read scaling, failover), but the leader is a write bottleneck/SPOF until failover. Leaderless (Dynamo/Cassandra): any node takes writes, replicated to N nodes with read/write quorums resolving consistency — highly available, no single leader, but more complex conflict handling.

nosql-sharding-replication.q: What does replication buy you, and what's the cost?
nosql-sharding-replication.a: Availability/fault tolerance (survive a node loss) and read scaling (spread reads across replicas). The cost is storage (N copies) and keeping copies consistent — replicas can diverge, forcing a consistency choice (sync vs async, quorums). More replicas mean more durability but more coordination and latency.

nosql-lsm.q: B-tree vs LSM-tree storage engines — trade-offs?
nosql-lsm.a: B-trees (most relational DBs) update in place → fast reads and good random access, but writes do random I/O. LSM-trees (Cassandra, RocksDB) buffer writes in a memtable and flush sequential immutable SSTables → very fast writes (sequential I/O), great for write-heavy loads, but reads may check several SSTables (mitigated by bloom filters) and need background compaction. Write-heavy → LSM; read-heavy/random → B-tree.

nosql-lsm.q: What is compaction in an LSM tree, and why is it needed?
nosql-lsm.a: LSM writes create many immutable SSTables (and tombstones for deletes); compaction merges them in the background, discarding overwritten/deleted data and keeping reads from degrading. It's the cost of fast writes — periodic background work that reclaims space and bounds how many files a read must check.

nosql-lsm.q: What is write amplification, and which engine has more?
nosql-lsm.a: Write amplification is writing the same data to disk multiple times beyond the logical write. LSM-trees amplify via compaction (data is rewritten as SSTables merge); B-trees amplify via page splits and in-place updates. It matters for SSD wear and throughput — a key tuning consideration in write-heavy systems.

nosql-consistency-tuning.q: What is a quorum and the R + W > N rule?
nosql-consistency-tuning.a: With N replicas, require W to acknowledge a write and R to respond to a read. If R + W > N, the read and write sets overlap, so a read is guaranteed to see the latest write (strong consistency). You tune R and W to trade consistency vs availability/latency — W=1 R=1 is fast but eventually consistent; W=quorum R=quorum is strong.

nosql-consistency-tuning.q: Strong vs eventual consistency — when each?
nosql-consistency-tuning.a: Strong = every read sees the latest write (more latency/coordination) — needed for balances, inventory, anything where stale equals wrong. Eventual = replicas converge over time and reads may be briefly stale (fast, highly available) — fine for likes, view counts, feeds. Tunable systems let you choose per operation.

nosql-consistency-tuning.q: How do leaderless stores handle conflicting concurrent writes?
nosql-consistency-tuning.a: Mechanisms like last-write-wins (by timestamp — can lose data), vector clocks (detect concurrency and surface conflicts to the app), or CRDTs (types that merge deterministically), plus read-repair and anti-entropy/hinted handoff to converge replicas. The app often must reason about conflict resolution.

nosql-timeseries.q: What makes a time-series database different?
nosql-timeseries.a: It's optimized for timestamped, append-mostly data with very high write rates and time-range queries: time-partitioned storage, efficient compression of sequential values, downsampling/rollups, and retention/TTL to age out old data. Examples: InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Prometheus — for IoT, metrics, and monitoring.

nosql-timeseries.q: Why not just use a relational DB for metrics?
nosql-timeseries.a: You can (TimescaleDB is Postgres-based), but generic relational storage isn't tuned for the firehose of timestamped writes, time-bucketed aggregations, automatic downsampling, and retention that monitoring needs. A purpose-built TSDB handles ingestion rate, compression, and time-range queries far more efficiently at scale.

nosql-timeseries.q: What are downsampling and retention in a TSDB?
nosql-timeseries.a: Downsampling aggregates fine-grained data into coarser rollups over time (1s → 1m → 1h) so old data stays useful but small; retention/TTL deletes raw data past an age. Together they bound storage growth while keeping historical trends — essential for metrics that arrive forever.

nosql-search-vector.q: How does a search engine (Elasticsearch) differ from a database?
nosql-search-vector.a: It builds an inverted index (term → documents) for fast full-text search, relevance ranking, fuzzy matching, and text aggregations — things relational LIKE queries do poorly. It's optimized for search/analytics on text, not transactional integrity, and is usually used alongside a primary DB (the DB is source of truth; search is a queryable index).

nosql-search-vector.q: What is a vector database and what problem does it solve?
nosql-search-vector.a: It stores embeddings (vectors) and does approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search to find semantically similar items — powering semantic search, recommendations, and RAG for LLMs. Instead of exact keyword match, it finds 'closest in meaning' by vector distance. Examples: Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector, Milvus.

nosql-search-vector.q: What is ANN and why approximate?
nosql-search-vector.a: Approximate Nearest Neighbor search finds the vectors closest to a query without scanning them all — exact search over millions/billions of high-dimensional vectors is too slow. ANN (HNSW, IVF) trades a tiny bit of recall for massive speed via clever index structures. It's what makes vector search practical at scale.

nosql-redis.a: The app checks the cache first; on a miss it reads the DB then writes the value into the cache for next time; on a write it updates the DB and invalidates or updates the cache. The cache holds hot data to cut DB load and latency. It's the most common caching pattern — the app manages the cache explicitly.

nosql-redis.q: What is cache invalidation and why is it hard?
nosql-redis.a: Keeping the cache consistent with the source of truth when data changes — you must invalidate or update cached entries or serve stale data. It's hard because of races (update DB then cache, or vice versa), TTL tuning (too long = stale, too short = thrashing), and distributed caches. It's famously one of the two hard problems in computing.

nosql-redis.q: Beyond caching, what else is Redis used for?
nosql-redis.a: As an in-memory data-structure store: rate limiting and counters (INCR), leaderboards (sorted sets), pub/sub messaging, session storage, distributed locks, and queues (lists/streams). Its speed and rich data types make it a Swiss-army knife well beyond a simple cache.

nosql-modeling.q: How is data modeling in NoSQL different from relational?
nosql-modeling.a: Relational models the data normalized, then you query it any way (joins, ad-hoc). NoSQL is query-first: you design tables/documents AROUND your known access patterns (one structure per query), often denormalizing and duplicating data, because there are no joins and you must hit the partition key. You optimize for reads you know, not flexibility you might need.

nosql-modeling.q: Why denormalize and duplicate data in NoSQL?
nosql-modeling.a: Without joins, you embed/duplicate related data so a single read serves a query (e.g., store the user's name inside each order document). It trades storage and update complexity (a name change touches many copies) for fast single-lookup reads at scale. Duplication is normal and intentional in NoSQL.

nosql-modeling.q: What happens if you get the partition key wrong?
nosql-modeling.a: Poor partition keys cause hot partitions (one node overloaded), inefficient queries (scatter-gather across all nodes), or unbounded partitions. Since you can't easily re-query a different way, a wrong key often means redesigning and migrating. Getting it right — high cardinality, even distribution, matches the query — is the most important NoSQL design decision.

bd-what-is-big-data.a: The classic 3 Vs: Volume (too big for one machine), Velocity (arriving fast/streaming), Variety (structured/semi/unstructured) — later Veracity and Value. Practically, data is 'big' when it outgrows a single machine's memory/disk/CPU, forcing distributed storage and processing. It's relative, not an absolute size.

bd-what-is-big-data.q: Why does big data require distributed systems?
bd-what-is-big-data.a: When data and computation exceed one machine, you spread them across many nodes for scale (parallel processing), fault tolerance (survive failures at scale), and cost (commodity hardware). This introduces partitioning, replication, and the network as the bottleneck — the core distributed concepts.

Scale up (vertical) = a bigger single machine — simple but capped and expensive. Scale out (horizontal) = more machines working together — the big-data approach: near-limitless scale on commodity hardware, but you must handle distribution (partitioning, coordination, failures). Big data is fundamentally about scaling out.

bd-spark-model.a: A driver (runs your program, builds the DAG, schedules) coordinates executors (workers that run tasks and hold data partitions in memory) on a cluster, via a cluster manager (YARN/Kubernetes/standalone). DataFrame ops build a lazy plan; an action triggers the driver to schedule tasks across executors. The unit of parallelism is the partition.

bd-spark-model.q: What is the shuffle and why does it dominate performance?
bd-spark-model.a: The shuffle redistributes data across the cluster (for joins, group-bys, repartitions) by writing intermediate data to disk and sending it over the network — far slower than in-memory compute. Since the network is the bottleneck, minimizing shuffles (filter early, broadcast small tables, avoid wide transforms) is the heart of Spark tuning.

bd-spark-model.q: Why is Spark lazy, and what's the benefit?
bd-spark-model.a: Transformations build a logical plan (DAG) but don't execute until an action. Laziness lets the Catalyst optimizer see the whole pipeline and optimize it — pushing down filters, pruning columns, combining steps, choosing join strategies — before running anything, rather than executing step-by-step.

bd-transformations-actions.a: Transformations (select, filter, join, groupBy) are lazy — they build the DAG and return a new DataFrame without executing. Actions (count, collect, write, show) trigger execution of the whole plan. Nothing runs until an action — that's lazy evaluation.

Narrow (map, filter) — each output partition depends on one input partition; no data movement, pipelined within a stage. Wide (groupBy, join, repartition) — output partitions depend on many inputs, requiring a shuffle (a stage boundary). Wide transformations are the expensive ones; minimizing them is key to performance.

bd-transformations-actions.q: Why can calling an action twice recompute everything, and how do you avoid it?
bd-transformations-actions.a: Spark recomputes the DAG from source on each action (it doesn't keep intermediate results by default). If you reuse a DataFrame across multiple actions, cache()/persist() it to materialize it once. Forgetting to cache reused data is a common cause of slow jobs.

bd-partitions-shuffle.q: What is data skew in Spark and how do you fix it?
bd-partitions-shuffle.a: Skew is uneven partition sizes (one key dominates), so one task runs far longer and may OOM — the job runs at the slowest task's speed. Fixes: salt the skewed key (random prefix to spread it), enable AQE skew-join handling, broadcast the small side, or repartition on a better key. Even partitioning is what makes parallelism real.

bd-partitions-shuffle.q: How many partitions should you have, and why does it matter?
bd-partitions-shuffle.a: Enough to use all cores with some over-subscription (a common rule: 2-4x the total cores), partitions sized ~100-200MB. Too few → underutilized cluster and huge tasks (OOM); too many → scheduling overhead and tiny tasks. spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default 200) controls post-shuffle partitioning — tune it to the data size.

bd-partitions-shuffle.q: What triggers a shuffle, and how do you reduce shuffles?
bd-partitions-shuffle.a: Wide operations: joins, groupBy/aggregations, distinct, repartition, and window functions. Reduce them by filtering/aggregating early (less data to shuffle), broadcasting small tables (avoid shuffling both sides), pre-partitioning by the join key, and avoiding unnecessary repartitions. Fewer/smaller shuffles = faster jobs.

bd-dataframes-sql.q: RDD vs DataFrame — which and why?
bd-dataframes-sql.a: DataFrames are the default: a structured, columnar API with a schema that Catalyst and the Tungsten engine optimize (predicate pushdown, code generation), and they're far faster and more memory-efficient than RDDs. Use RDDs only for low-level control or unstructured data. DataFrame/Dataset beats RDD for almost everything.

bd-dataframes-sql.q: What is Catalyst and how does it help?
bd-dataframes-sql.a: Catalyst is Spark SQL's query optimizer. It takes your DataFrame/SQL, builds a logical plan, applies rule- and cost-based optimizations (predicate pushdown, column pruning, constant folding, join reordering), and generates an optimized physical plan plus efficient bytecode (Tungsten codegen). It's why DataFrames beat hand-written RDD code.

bd-dataframes-sql.q: Is Spark SQL different from the DataFrame API in performance?
bd-dataframes-sql.a: No — both compile to the same Catalyst-optimized plan, so SQL and DataFrame code perform identically. Choose by readability: SQL for set-based logic analysts know, the DataFrame API for programmatic composition. They're interchangeable and equally optimized.

bd-mapreduce.a: A two-phase model: Map applies a function to each input record emitting key-value pairs; the framework shuffles/sorts pairs by key; Reduce aggregates values per key. Classic example: word count (map emits (word,1), reduce sums per word). It expresses computation that scales across a cluster by moving compute to the data.

bd-mapreduce.q: What's the role of the shuffle/sort in MapReduce?
bd-mapreduce.a: Between map and reduce, the framework groups all values for the same key (partition by key, sort) and routes them to the right reducer — the data-movement phase. It's the expensive, network-heavy step (same idea as Spark's shuffle), which is why minimizing data between map and reduce (combiners) matters.

bd-mapreduce.q: What is a combiner and why use it?
bd-mapreduce.a: A combiner is a mini-reducer that runs on the map output locally (before the shuffle) to pre-aggregate, reducing data sent over the network. For word count, it sums counts per word on each mapper first. It cuts shuffle volume dramatically when the reduce is associative/commutative (like sum).

bd-spark-vs-mapreduce.q: Why is Spark faster than MapReduce?
bd-spark-vs-mapreduce.a: MapReduce writes intermediate results to disk (HDFS) between every map and reduce stage — heavy I/O. Spark keeps intermediate data in memory across stages and uses a DAG scheduler that pipelines operations without writing to disk between them, plus Catalyst optimization. For iterative/multi-stage jobs, often 10-100x faster.

Largely yes for new development — Spark is faster, has higher-level APIs (DataFrames/SQL), and unifies batch, streaming, and ML. MapReduce persists in legacy Hadoop and is conceptually important (its map/shuffle/reduce model underlies Spark's execution), but you'd rarely write new MapReduce jobs.

bd-spark-vs-mapreduce.q: What did Spark keep from MapReduce?
bd-spark-vs-mapreduce.a: The core distributed-execution idea — partition data, run computation in parallel close to the data, shuffle by key when needed, and recover from failures by recomputing. Spark generalizes map/reduce into an in-memory DAG of operations, but the partition/shuffle/fault-tolerance fundamentals are inherited.

bd-join-strategies.a: Broadcast hash join — ship a small table to every executor and join locally (no shuffle of the big side; fastest when one side fits in memory). Sort-merge join — sort both sides by key and merge (default for two large tables; requires a shuffle). Shuffle-hash join — shuffle both, build a hash on one side. The optimizer picks by size; you can hint broadcast.

bd-join-strategies.q: When and why use a broadcast join?
bd-join-strategies.a: When one side is small enough to fit in each executor's memory (below spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold, default ~10MB). Broadcasting it avoids shuffling the large table entirely — a massive speedup. Force it with broadcast(df). The risk: broadcasting something too large causes OOM, so size it carefully.

bd-join-strategies.q: How does AQE improve joins at runtime?
bd-join-strategies.a: Adaptive Query Execution re-optimizes using actual runtime statistics: it can switch a sort-merge join to a broadcast join if a side turns out small, coalesce shuffle partitions, and split skewed partitions (skew-join handling). It fixes bad plans that static, estimate-based optimization would have chosen.

bd-aqe-tuning.a: A Spark 3.x feature (default-on) that re-optimizes the plan during execution using real runtime statistics — dynamically coalescing shuffle partitions, switching join strategies (e.g., to broadcast), and handling skew by splitting large partitions. It corrects the optimizer's estimate-based mistakes with actual data sizes.

bd-aqe-tuning.q: Walk through tuning a slow Spark job.
bd-aqe-tuning.a: Check the Spark UI for skew (one long task), spill (memory pressure), and shuffle size. Then: filter/prune early, broadcast small joins, fix skew (salting/AQE), right-size partitions (spark.sql.shuffle.partitions), cache reused DataFrames, use efficient formats (Parquet), and avoid UDFs (use built-in functions). Address the biggest stage first.

bd-aqe-tuning.q: What is spill, and why is it bad?
bd-aqe-tuning.a: Spill is when a task's data exceeds executor memory and Spark writes it to disk (during shuffles/aggregations/sorts). It's slow (disk + serialization) and signals memory pressure. Fix by increasing partitions (smaller tasks), more executor memory, reducing data via earlier filtering/aggregation, or fixing skew. Watch spill metrics in the UI.

bd-structured-streaming.q: How does Spark Structured Streaming work?
bd-structured-streaming.a: It treats a stream as an unbounded table that grows with new data; you write the same DataFrame/SQL as for batch, and Spark runs it incrementally as micro-batches (or continuous), maintaining state and outputting via a sink. It provides exactly-once semantics with checkpointing and event-time windowing — batch-like code for streams.

bd-structured-streaming.q: Micro-batch vs continuous, and how does it compare to Flink?
bd-structured-streaming.a: Spark's default is micro-batch (process small batches per trigger — low-to-moderate latency, high throughput); continuous mode offers lower latency. Flink is true event-at-a-time streaming with lower latency and richer state/event-time handling. Spark wins for one engine across batch + streaming; Flink for latency-critical, complex streaming.

bd-structured-streaming.q: What do watermarks do in streaming?
bd-structured-streaming.a: A watermark tells the engine how late data can arrive, so it knows when a time window is 'complete', can emit results, and can drop old state. It bounds the state kept for late events and enables correct event-time aggregations despite out-of-order data. Without it, state grows unbounded.

bd-hadoop-ecosystem.a: HDFS is the Hadoop Distributed File System — stores huge files as blocks replicated (x3) across nodes for fault tolerance, with a NameNode (metadata) and DataNodes (blocks). YARN is the resource manager — it schedules and allocates cluster resources (containers) to jobs. Storage (HDFS) + resource management (YARN) + compute (MapReduce/Spark) form classic Hadoop.

The MapReduce compute engine is largely superseded by Spark, and HDFS by cloud object storage (S3/GCS). But the ecosystem's ideas live on (distributed storage, YARN/Kubernetes resource management, the Hive metastore, the lakehouse). Many enterprises still run Hadoop; new builds favor cloud object storage + Spark/Trino + a table format.

bd-hadoop-ecosystem.q: Why did cloud object storage replace HDFS for many?
bd-hadoop-ecosystem.a: Object storage (S3/GCS) decouples storage from compute (scale and pay for each independently), is cheaper and infinitely scalable, fully managed (no NameNode to operate), and durable — versus HDFS which couples storage to the cluster and needs operational care. The lakehouse (table format on object storage) is the modern successor.

bd-flink-trino.a: Flink is a true streaming engine — processes events one at a time with low latency, rich stateful processing, event-time windows, and exactly-once — built streaming-first. Spark Structured Streaming is micro-batch (small batches), slightly higher latency but unifying batch + streaming in one engine. Choose Flink for latency-critical/complex streaming, Spark for one-engine simplicity.

bd-flink-trino.q: What is Trino (Presto) and when use it?
bd-flink-trino.a: A distributed SQL query engine with NO storage — it queries data where it lives (lake/Parquet, RDBMS, warehouse) and can federate across sources in one query. It's optimized for fast interactive/ad-hoc analytics. Use it for low-latency SQL over the lake or to join across systems; Spark is better for heavy ETL/transformations.

bd-flink-trino.q: Spark vs Flink vs Trino — one line each?
bd-flink-trino.a: Spark = general-purpose distributed compute (batch + micro-batch streaming + ML). Flink = streaming-first, low-latency, stateful event processing. Trino = interactive federated SQL over data in place. They overlap but optimize for different points: ETL, real-time, and ad-hoc query.

bd-hive.q: What is Hive and the metastore?
bd-hive.a: Hive provides SQL (HiveQL) over data in HDFS/object storage, originally compiling to MapReduce (now Tez/Spark). The Hive Metastore is the crucial part: a catalog of table definitions (schema, partitions, file locations) that lets many engines (Spark, Trino, Presto) share the same tables over the same files. The metastore outlived Hive's execution engine.

bd-hive.q: Why does the metastore matter beyond Hive?
bd-hive.a: It decouples table metadata from any single engine — Spark, Trino, and others read the same catalog to know what tables exist, their schema, and where the files are. It's the foundation of 'SQL on the lake' and the precursor to modern catalogs (Glue, Unity Catalog). One catalog, many engines, same files.

bd-hive.q: What is partitioning in Hive and why?
bd-hive.a: Storing a table's data in subdirectories by a column (e.g., dt=2024-01-01/) so queries filtering on that column read only the relevant directories (partition pruning) — slashing data scanned. It's the same idea as partitioned Parquet in the lake; a good partition column (often date) is key to performance.

bd-scala.q: Scala vs PySpark — does it matter for performance?
bd-scala.a: For DataFrame/SQL operations, no meaningful difference — both compile to the same Catalyst-optimized JVM plan. The gap appears with Python UDFs (data serializes between the JVM and a Python process — slow); Scala/Java UDFs run natively. Use PySpark for productivity; prefer built-in functions or Scala UDFs on hot paths.

bd-scala.q: When would you choose Scala over PySpark?
bd-scala.a: When you need maximum performance with custom logic (native UDFs without serialization overhead), tight JVM-ecosystem integration, type safety (Datasets), or you're building Spark libraries. PySpark wins for the data-science ecosystem, faster development, and team familiarity — most teams use it. Scala for performance-critical/typed code.

bd-scala.q: Why are Python UDFs slow in Spark?
bd-scala.a: Spark runs on the JVM; a Python UDF serializes each row from the JVM to a Python worker, executes Python, and serializes back — crossing the process boundary per row, and it breaks Catalyst optimization. Mitigate with built-in functions (always prefer), or pandas/vectorized UDFs (Arrow-based, batched) when a UDF is unavoidable.

bd-rdd.q: What is an RDD and what does 'resilient' mean?
bd-rdd.a: A Resilient Distributed Dataset — Spark's low-level immutable, partitioned collection. 'Resilient' means it tracks its lineage (the transformations that built it), so if a partition is lost to a node failure, Spark recomputes it from the lineage rather than replicating data. That lineage-based recovery is Spark's fault-tolerance foundation.

bd-rdd.q: When would you still use RDDs over DataFrames?
bd-rdd.a: Rarely — for low-level control over partitioning/computation, unstructured data without a schema, or custom operations not expressible in the DataFrame API. DataFrames are preferred because Catalyst can't optimize opaque RDD code. Most code should use DataFrames/Datasets; RDDs are the foundation underneath.

bd-rdd.q: How does Spark recover from a lost partition?
bd-rdd.a: Via lineage: each RDD/DataFrame records how it was derived from its parents. If an executor dies and a partition is lost, Spark recomputes just that partition from its lineage (re-running the transformations on the source), not the whole job — avoiding the cost of replicating intermediate data while staying fault-tolerant.

bd-udf-memory.q: How is Spark executor memory organized?
bd-udf-memory.a: Executor memory splits into execution memory (shuffles, joins, sorts, aggregations) and storage memory (cached data) — unified and able to borrow from each other — plus user memory (your structures) and reserved memory. Too little leads to spill (to disk) or OOM. Tuning executor memory, cores, and partition count balances this.

Built-in Spark SQL functions are Catalyst-optimized and run in the JVM with codegen; UDFs (especially Python) are opaque to the optimizer (no pushdown) and, for Python, incur per-row JVM-to-Python serialization. So UDFs are slower and block optimization. Try built-in functions first; use pandas/vectorized UDFs if you must.

bd-udf-memory.q: What causes OutOfMemory errors in Spark and how do you fix them?
bd-udf-memory.a: Common causes: data skew (one huge partition), collecting too much to the driver (collect() on a big DataFrame), too few partitions (huge tasks), or large broadcasts. Fixes: increase partitions, fix skew (salting/AQE), avoid collect() (write instead), size broadcasts, and raise executor/driver memory. Diagnose via the Spark UI's memory/spill metrics.

bd-submit-modes.q: Client vs cluster deploy mode in spark-submit?
bd-submit-modes.a: In client mode the driver runs on the submitting machine (good for interactive/dev, but that machine must stay up). In cluster mode the driver runs inside the cluster (on a worker), so the submitting machine can disconnect — preferred for production. Executors always run in the cluster either way.

It launches an application with resources and settings: --master (cluster manager), --deploy-mode, --num-executors / --executor-cores / --executor-memory / --driver-memory, --conf for Spark settings, plus the app jar/py and args. Right-sizing executors and memory here is core to performance and cost.

bd-submit-modes.q: Which cluster managers can Spark run on?
bd-submit-modes.a: Standalone (Spark's own), YARN (Hadoop), Kubernetes (increasingly common in the cloud), and historically Mesos. The cluster manager allocates resources (executor containers) to the application. Managed services (Databricks, EMR, Dataproc) wrap these so you don't manage clusters directly.

bd-pyspark.q: Walk through a typical PySpark ETL job.
bd-pyspark.a: Create a SparkSession; read source data (spark.read.parquet/csv with a schema); transform with the DataFrame API (filter, withColumn, join, groupBy/agg) — all lazy; optionally cache reused frames; then write the result (df.write.partitionBy(...).parquet(...)), which triggers execution. Tune partitions, broadcast small joins, prefer built-in functions, and check the Spark UI.

bd-pyspark.q: How do you read only the data you need in PySpark?
bd-pyspark.a: Use columnar Parquet with predicate pushdown and partition pruning — select only needed columns (Spark prunes them) and filter on partition columns so Spark skips whole directories/row groups. Push filters as early as possible so less data is read and shuffled. Reading less is the cheapest optimization.

Both materialize a DataFrame so it's computed once and reused across actions. cache() is shorthand for persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK); persist() lets you choose the storage level (memory-only, disk, serialized, replicated). Use them when a DataFrame is reused multiple times, and unpersist when done to free memory.

pipe-etl-elt.q: ETL vs ELT — difference and why ELT won?
pipe-etl-elt.a: ETL transforms on a separate engine before loading the result; ELT loads raw into the warehouse/lake first, then transforms in place with its compute.
