Catch them with automated checks at every boundary AND observability (freshness/volume/distribution monitoring) that flags anomalies you didn't anticipate. You can't wait for an exception that never comes.

gov-pii-security.q: What is PII and how should a data platform handle it?
gov-pii-security.a: Personally Identifiable Information — data that identifies a person (name, email, SSN, location). Handle it with classification (know where it lives), minimization (collect/retain only what's needed), access control (least privilege, RBAC), masking/tokenization/encryption, audit logging, and honoring privacy rights (deletion/consent). Treat PII as high-sensitivity throughout the lifecycle, by design.

gov-pii-security.q: What are the layers of data security?
gov-pii-security.a: Defense in depth: access control (IAM, least privilege, RBAC), encryption (at rest with KMS, in transit with TLS), masking/tokenization (hide sensitive values), network isolation (private subnets/endpoints), audit logging (who accessed what), and governance (classification, retention, lineage). No single control suffices; layer them.

gov-pii-security.q: How do you balance data access with security?
gov-pii-security.a: Use role-/attribute-based access control for least privilege (each role sees only what it needs), column/row-level security and dynamic masking so sensitive fields hide from unauthorized users while the table stays usable, and a governed catalog to manage and audit grants. Maximize useful access while minimizing exposure — secure by default, opened deliberately.

gov-lineage-catalog.q: What is data lineage and why does it matter?
gov-lineage-catalog.a: Lineage tracks data's journey — where it came from, how it was transformed, and what depends on it (table- and column-level). It matters for impact analysis (what breaks if I change this?), debugging (where did this wrong number originate?), compliance/audit (prove how a figure was derived), and trust (consumers see provenance). It's the map of how data flows.

gov-lineage-catalog.q: What is a data catalog and what problem does it solve?
gov-lineage-catalog.a: A searchable inventory of data assets — tables, schemas, owners, descriptions, lineage, classifications, quality/freshness — so people can DISCOVER and TRUST data. It solves the 'I don't know what data exists or whether I can trust it' problem in large orgs, enabling self-service analytics and governance. Examples: DataHub, Amundsen, Unity Catalog, Collibra.

Automatically by parsing transformation logic (dbt generates lineage from ref()s; parsers extract column lineage from SQL), via orchestrator metadata (which task produced which table), and through catalog integrations, with manual docs supplementing. Automated, column-level lineage is the goal because manual lineage rots — and it powers impact analysis and debugging.

gov-privacy-compliance.q: What rights does GDPR give data subjects, and what does that mean for pipelines?
gov-privacy-compliance.a: Access (provide their data), erasure / right-to-be-forgotten (delete on request), portability (export usable format), rectification, and consent/lawful-basis. For pipelines: you must FIND all of a person's data (lineage/catalog), DELETE it across systems including derived tables (hard with immutable lakes — MERGE/delete + propagate), honor consent, and minimize collection. Design for deletion and traceability from the start.

gov-privacy-compliance.q: How do you implement 'right to be forgotten' on a data lake?
gov-privacy-compliance.a: It's hard because lake files are immutable and data is copied widely. Use a table format (Delta/Iceberg) supporting MERGE/DELETE to physically remove rows; track the person's data via lineage to find every copy (raw, silver, gold, exports); delete/anonymize across all; handle backups/retention; or crypto-shred (destroy the subject's encryption key). You must prove complete deletion.

gov-privacy-compliance.q: What is data minimization and why does it reduce risk?
gov-privacy-compliance.a: Collecting and retaining only the personal data you actually need, for only as long as needed. It reduces risk because data you don't hold can't be breached, misused, or subject to deletion requests — less PII = smaller attack surface, lower compliance burden, lower cost. It's a core privacy-by-design principle and a GDPR requirement.

gov-access-masking.q: Explain RBAC vs ABAC and column/row-level security.
gov-access-masking.a: RBAC grants permissions by ROLE (analyst, engineer); ABAC by ATTRIBUTES (department, clearance, data tags) for finer dynamic control. Column-level security restricts which columns a user sees (hide SSN); row-level security filters which rows (a regional manager sees only their region). Together they let one table safely serve many users — each sees only what they're entitled to.

Masking obscures data for display (show ****1234), often irreversible for the viewer. Tokenization replaces sensitive values with reversible tokens (mapping stored securely) so analytics run without exposing the real value. Encryption transforms data with a key (reversible with it) to protect at rest/in transit. Masking for display, tokenization to use without exposing, encryption to protect storage/transport.

Masking applied AT QUERY TIME based on the user's role — the underlying data is unchanged, but unauthorized users see masked values (***) while authorized users see the real data, from the same table/query. It lets you keep one copy and enforce per-user visibility without separate masked copies — great for serving sensitive tables to mixed audiences.

gov-data-mesh.q: What is data mesh and what problem does it address?
gov-data-mesh.a: A decentralized organizational approach: domain teams OWN their data as PRODUCTS (with quality, SLAs, contracts, discoverability), supported by a self-serve platform and federated governance. It addresses the bottleneck and lack of domain knowledge of a central data team — scaling data by distributing ownership to those who know it, while keeping interoperability via standards.

gov-data-mesh.q: What are the four principles of data mesh?
gov-data-mesh.a: (1) Domain-oriented ownership — domains own their data. (2) Data as a product — datasets have quality, docs, SLAs, consumers. (3) Self-serve data platform — infra that lets domains build/serve products without deep platform expertise. (4) Federated computational governance — global standards (interop, security, contracts) enforced automatically while domains stay autonomous.

gov-data-mesh.q: What are the risks/criticisms of data mesh?
gov-data-mesh.a: It's an organizational shift, not just tech — risks include inconsistent quality across domains, duplicated effort, fragmented governance, and high coordination overhead if standards aren't enforced. It suits large orgs with many domains and a mature platform; smaller orgs are simpler with a central team. Data contracts and federated governance keep mesh from devolving into silos.

gov-mdm.q: What is Master Data Management and the golden record?
gov-mdm.a: MDM creates a single, authoritative, consistent version of core business entities (customer, product, supplier) — the 'golden record' — by matching, merging, and de-duplicating records scattered across source systems (CRM, billing, support) that each hold a slightly different version. It resolves conflicts into one trusted source of truth per entity, so reports and operations agree on 'who is this customer'.

gov-mdm.q: How does MDM match and merge records?
gov-mdm.a: Through entity resolution: matching records that refer to the same real-world entity via deterministic rules (same email/ID) and probabilistic/fuzzy matching (similar names, addresses), then merging via survivorship rules (which source/value wins per field — most recent, most trusted). The result is a deduplicated golden record linked back to sources. It's hard because real-world data is messy and inconsistent.

gov-mdm.q: Why is MDM important for analytics?
gov-mdm.a: Without it, the same customer appears as multiple records across systems, so counts, revenue, and segmentation are wrong (double-counting, fragmented histories). MDM gives one consistent entity definition so analytics, reporting, and personalization are accurate and reconcilable across the business — the foundation for trustworthy entity-level metrics.

gov-encryption.q: Encryption at rest vs in transit, and key management?
gov-encryption.a: At rest = data encrypted on disk/storage (stolen disks/buckets are useless) with keys managed by a KMS. In transit = data encrypted over the network (TLS/HTTPS) against interception. Key management (KMS) generates, stores, rotates, and access-controls keys — the keys are the crown jewels, protected, rotated, and audited separately from the data. Both layers are standard practice.

gov-encryption.q: What is envelope encryption and crypto-shredding?
gov-encryption.a: Envelope encryption: encrypt data with a data key, then encrypt that key with a master key (in KMS) — efficient and lets you rotate master keys without re-encrypting all data. Crypto-shredding: to 'delete' data, destroy its encryption key so the ciphertext is permanently unreadable — useful for honoring deletion/RTBF on immutable storage where physically deleting every copy is hard.

gov-encryption.q: Why is key management more important than the encryption algorithm?
gov-encryption.a: Modern algorithms (AES) are effectively unbreakable; the weak point is the KEYS — if leaked, hard-coded, unrotated, or over-permissioned, the encryption is worthless. So key management (a KMS: secure storage, rotation, least-privilege access, audit, separation from data) is where security actually lives. Protecting and governing keys is the real job.

gov-compliance-frameworks.a: GDPR — EU regulation protecting personal-data privacy and subject rights (applies to anyone handling EU residents' data). HIPAA — US law protecting health information (PHI) for healthcare entities. SOC 2 — a security/availability AUDIT framework (a report attesting your controls), common for B2B SaaS trust. GDPR/HIPAA are legal regulations on specific data types; SOC 2 is a voluntary attestation of controls. Know which apply to your data and customers.

gov-compliance-frameworks.q: How do compliance requirements shape data engineering?
gov-compliance-frameworks.a: They mandate controls you must build in: access control and audit logging, encryption, classification and retention, deletion/consent capabilities (GDPR), data residency (where data is stored), and lineage to prove provenance. Compliance isn't bolted on — it shapes architecture (regional storage, deletion-capable table formats, governed catalogs). Engineer for the regulations that apply.

Legal requirements that data about a country's citizens be stored and processed within specific geographic boundaries (e.g., EU data stays in the EU). It shapes region selection for storage and compute, replication, and cross-border transfer rules. You design the platform's regions and data flows to comply — a key constraint for global companies.

gov-classification-retention.q: What is data classification and why do it?
gov-classification-retention.a: Categorizing data by sensitivity (public, internal, confidential, restricted) so you apply controls — access, encryption, masking, retention, audit — proportional to risk. You can't protect what you haven't categorized; classification drives every downstream governance decision (who sees it, how it's stored, how long you keep it). It's the foundation of a governance program.

gov-classification-retention.q: What is a data retention policy and why does it matter?
gov-classification-retention.a: Rules for how long data is kept and when it's deleted, by type/classification (logs 90 days, financial records 7 years, then delete). It matters for compliance (regulations mandate both minimum retention AND deletion), cost (don't store forever), and risk (data you don't hold can't be breached or subpoenaed). Legal holds pause deletion for litigation. Automate it.

gov-classification-retention.q: How does minimizing retained data reduce risk?
gov-classification-retention.a: Less data stored means a smaller attack surface, fewer subjects for deletion requests, lower compliance scope, and lower cost. Privacy-by-design and regulations push 'keep only what you need, for only as long as you need it'. Retention policies + minimization aren't just cleanup — they're a security and compliance control. The safest data is the data you don't keep.

perf-mindset.q: What's the right approach to optimizing a slow pipeline?
perf-mindset.a: Measure first — profile to find the actual bottleneck (don't guess), fix the BIGGEST one, then re-measure (the bottleneck moves). By Amdahl's law, optimizing a stage that's 5% of runtime barely helps; find the dominant cost (often a shuffle, full scan, or skew) and attack that. Data-driven and iterative, avoiding premature optimization.

perf-mindset.q: What is Amdahl's law and why does it matter for optimization?
perf-mindset.a: The speedup from optimizing part of a system is limited by the fraction of total time that part takes — if a stage is 80% of runtime, even infinite speedup of the other 20% caps you at 1.25x. It tells you to optimize the DOMINANT bottleneck, not whatever's easiest. Profile to find where time actually goes.

Intuition about bottlenecks is often wrong — you can spend days optimizing the wrong thing. Profiling (query plans, Spark UI, timing) shows where time and cost actually go, so you fix the real bottleneck and verify the change helped. It prevents wasted effort and premature optimization.

perf-sql.q: How do you optimize a slow SQL query?
perf-sql.a: Read EXPLAIN/ANALYZE: full scans where an index should help, the join algorithm, bad row estimates (stale stats), the costliest node. Then add/adjust indexes, make predicates sargable, partition/cluster to prune, update statistics, select only needed columns, and rewrite (avoid correlated subqueries). Fix the biggest cost first.

perf-sql.q: What is a sargable predicate and why does it matter?
perf-sql.a: A predicate that can use an index (Search ARGument ABLE). Wrapping the column in a function (WHERE YEAR(d)=2024) or a leading wildcard (LIKE '%x') makes it non-sargable → full scan. Rewrite as a range (d >= '2024-01-01' AND d < '2025-01-01') so the index applies. Sargability is often the difference between a seek and a scan.

perf-sql.q: How does data layout affect query speed?
perf-sql.a: Massively — partitioning lets the engine skip whole partitions (prune by date), clustering/sorting + columnar stats let it skip files/row-groups (data skipping), and reading only needed columns cuts I/O. Since queries are usually I/O-bound, layout that minimizes data scanned is often the single biggest speedup — bigger than query tweaks.

perf-spark.q: What are the top Spark performance levers?
perf-spark.a: Minimize the shuffle (filter/aggregate early, broadcast small joins), fix data skew (salt/AQE), right-size partitions (~100-200MB, ~2-4x cores), cache reused DataFrames, read less (Parquet + column/partition pruning), avoid UDFs (use built-ins), and read the Spark UI for the longest stage/spill. The shuffle and skew are usually the biggest wins.

perf-spark.q: How do you diagnose a slow Spark job?
perf-spark.a: Open the Spark UI: find the longest stage, check for skew (one task far longer), spill (data exceeding memory → disk), and large shuffle reads/writes. Then attack the biggest: filter earlier, broadcast small joins, fix skew, increase partitions, cache reused frames. Diagnose with the UI before changing anything — measure, then optimize.

perf-spark.q: Why is minimizing data scanned the cheapest Spark optimization?
perf-spark.a: Spark jobs and cost are dominated by I/O and shuffle, both proportional to data volume. Reading less — via Parquet, column pruning, partition pruning, and early filters — reduces I/O, shuffle, and compute at once, with no code complexity. 'Don't read what you don't need' is the highest-ROI optimization before tuning anything else.

perf-layout.q: How do partitioning and file sizing affect performance?
perf-layout.a: Partitioning (by a low-cardinality common filter like date) lets queries skip whole partitions. File sizing matters: too many small files slow reads (per-file overhead, metadata) — the small-files problem — so you compact (OPTIMIZE) into ~128MB-1GB files. Right partitioning + right file sizes + columnar stats minimize data scanned, the dominant cost.

perf-layout.q: What is the small files problem and how do you fix it?
perf-layout.a: Streaming/frequent writes produce many tiny files; reading them is slow (per-file open cost, huge metadata, poor parallelism). Fix with compaction (OPTIMIZE / bin-packing) to merge into right-sized files, tuning write batch sizes, and merge-on-read formats that defer compaction. It's essential lakehouse maintenance for read performance.

Partition by a low-cardinality column commonly used in filters (date) → directory-level skipping. Cluster/Z-order by high-cardinality columns frequently queried (user_id) → co-locate within files so min/max stats skip data. Don't partition by high-cardinality (too many small partitions). Use both: partition on date, cluster on the hot query column.

perf-caching.q: When and how do you use caching/pre-computation for performance?
perf-caching.a: Cache hot, frequently-read, expensive-to-compute results closer to consumers — Redis for low-latency lookups, materialized views/aggregate tables for common rollups (precompute once), and Spark cache() for reused DataFrames within a job. It trades storage/staleness for speed. Use it for repeated reads of the same expensive computation; mind invalidation/freshness.

Speed vs freshness and complexity. A cache serves fast but may be stale until invalidated/refreshed, and you must handle invalidation (the hard part — races, TTLs). Pre-computed aggregates are fast but cost storage and refresh, and lag the source. Cache when reads are frequent and slight staleness is acceptable; don't cache rapidly-changing data needing strong consistency.

perf-caching.q: Aggregate/materialized tables vs caching — how do they relate?
perf-caching.a: Both are pre-computation for read speed. Materialized views/aggregate tables precompute query results in the warehouse (refreshed on schedule); a cache (Redis) stores hot key-value results in memory for low-latency app access. Use aggregates for analytical rollups, a cache for high-QPS point lookups. Both trade refresh cost/staleness for fast reads.

perf-cost.q: How do you make a data platform cost-efficient?
perf-cost.a: Cost ≈ compute-time × resources × data scanned, so: scan less (partition/prune, columnar, fewer columns), right-size compute, use spot/preemptible for fault-tolerant jobs, autoscale and kill idle clusters, tier/lifecycle storage, minimize egress, and cache/precompute to avoid repeat work. In per-scan warehouses, efficient queries directly cut cost. Measure cost per job/query and optimize the biggest spenders.

perf-cost.q: Why is 'scan less data' both a performance AND cost win?
perf-cost.a: Both runtime (I/O-bound) and cost (per-byte-scanned in serverless warehouses, or compute-time everywhere) are proportional to data volume. Partitioning, pruning, columnar formats, and early filters reduce data scanned — so the same change makes queries faster AND cheaper. Efficiency and cost control are the same lever.

perf-cost.q: How do you scale efficiently (not just scale up)?
perf-cost.a: Match resources to the workload: right-size instances (don't over-provision), scale horizontally with autoscaling so you pay for what you use, use spot for batch, separate storage from compute (transient clusters), and reduce the work itself (read less, precompute). Scaling efficiently means doing less work and provisioning elastically — not just bigger/more machines.

sd-framework.q: How do you approach any data pipeline / system design question?
sd-framework.a: A framework: (1) Clarify requirements (sources, volume/velocity, latency/SLA, consumers, constraints). (2) Sketch the high-level pipeline (ingest → store → transform → serve). (3) Deep-dive key components and choices (storage, engine, batch vs stream). (4) Address scale, cost, and trade-offs. (5) Wrap up (failure modes, monitoring, next steps). Think out loud; there's no single right answer.

sd-framework.q: Why start a design with clarifying questions?
sd-framework.a: The right design depends entirely on the requirements — latency (real-time vs daily?), scale (GB vs PB?), consumers (BI vs ML vs app?), and constraints (cost, stack, compliance). Jumping to a solution without clarifying leads to over- or under-engineering. Interviewers explicitly evaluate whether you gather requirements before designing.

sd-framework.q: What does a good pipeline design include beyond the happy path?
sd-framework.a: Failure handling (retries, idempotency, dead-letter), data quality/observability (tests, monitoring, SLAs), scalability and cost, and operability (orchestration, alerting, lineage). Senior designs address what happens when sources are late/malformed, how it scales, what it costs, and how you'd run and debug it — not just the data flow.

sd-batch-vs-stream-choice.q: How do you decide between batch and streaming?
sd-batch-vs-stream-choice.a: Let the LATENCY requirement decide: data needed within seconds (fraud, real-time dashboards) → streaming; minutes is fine → micro-batch; hourly/daily acceptable (most reporting) → batch (simpler, cheaper). Streaming adds cost and complexity (state, ordering, exactly-once), so don't stream what a daily batch can serve. Ask what freshness the use case truly needs.

sd-batch-vs-stream-choice.q: What's the cost of choosing streaming when you don't need it?
sd-batch-vs-stream-choice.a: Streaming requires always-on infrastructure, stateful processing, handling out-of-order/late data, exactly-once semantics, and harder debugging/reprocessing — more cost and operational complexity than a scheduled batch job. If the business only looks at the data daily, streaming wastes money and effort. Match the solution's latency to what the use case actually requires.

sd-batch-vs-stream-choice.q: Can you mix batch and streaming?
sd-batch-vs-stream-choice.a: Yes — Lambda (batch + speed layers) or, increasingly, Kappa (one streaming path, reprocess by replay) or a lakehouse doing both. Often you stream raw events for freshness AND batch-reprocess for accuracy/corrections — serving real-time approximate results from streaming and authoritative results from batch. Choose per use case, not dogmatically.

sd-capacity-estimation.q: Estimate storage and throughput for 50M events/day at 500 bytes each.
sd-capacity-estimation.a: 50M × 500 B = 25 GB/day → ~9 TB/year raw. Adjust: columnar compression (~5x) → ~1.8 TB/yr stored; replication (x2-3) for footprint; QPS = 50M / 86,400 ≈ 580 events/sec average (peak maybe 3-5x). State assumptions out loud (peak vs average, retention, compression). The method and reasoning matter more than the exact number.

sd-capacity-estimation.q: What back-of-envelope numbers should a DE know?
sd-capacity-estimation.a: Rough sizes (a row ~ hundreds of bytes; KB→MB→GB→TB ×1000), seconds/day (~86,400), columnar compression (~3-10x), replication (x2-3), and QPS = events/day ÷ 86,400 then × a peak factor. With these you can size storage, throughput, and rough cost quickly — a staple of system-design rounds.

sd-capacity-estimation.q: Why state assumptions during capacity estimation?
sd-capacity-estimation.a: The exact number depends on assumptions (peak vs average load, retention, compression, replication), and interviewers evaluate your reasoning, not a memorized figure. Stating assumptions makes your estimate auditable, shows you understand the levers, and lets the interviewer change a parameter and see if you can re-derive. The method is the point.

sd-tradeoffs.q: What are the core trade-offs in data system design?
sd-tradeoffs.a: Latency vs cost vs complexity (and accuracy/consistency). Lower latency (streaming) costs more and is more complex; stronger consistency costs availability/latency; more accuracy may need batch reprocessing. There's no free lunch — pick the point that fits the requirements and state the trade-off explicitly. Good design is choosing deliberately, not maximizing everything.

sd-tradeoffs.q: How do you reason about a latency vs cost trade-off?
sd-tradeoffs.a: Start from the requirement: what latency does the use case actually need? Then pick the cheapest solution that meets it — daily batch cheapest, micro-batch more, true streaming most. Don't pay for real-time if daily suffices. Quantify if possible (streaming infra cost vs business value of fresher data). Make the trade-off visible and justified by the requirement.

sd-tradeoffs.q: Give an example of a consistency vs availability trade-off.
sd-tradeoffs.a: A real-time inventory count: strong consistency means every read sees the latest write (more coordination/latency, may reject during partitions) — needed so you don't oversell. Eventual consistency is faster and more available but may briefly show stale counts. Choose based on whether stale data or unavailability is worse — the CAP/PACELC trade-off applied.

sd-worked-example.q: Design an analytics pipeline for a retailer's sales data.
sd-worked-example.a: Clarify (orders DB + clickstream; daily reporting + some real-time; scale). Ingest: CDC from orders + events to Kafka, or batch extracts → land raw in the lake (bronze). Transform: clean/model into a star schema (fact_sales + dims) via dbt/Spark (silver→gold) with data tests. Serve: warehouse/lakehouse for BI; aggregate tables for dashboards. Orchestrate (Airflow), add quality/observability and lineage. Address scale (partition by date), cost (columnar, prune), and failure handling (idempotent, retries).

sd-worked-example.q: What components does an end-to-end batch analytics pipeline need?
sd-worked-example.a: Ingestion (batch extract or CDC), raw storage (lake bronze), transformation (clean + dimensional model in silver/gold via dbt/Spark), a serving layer (warehouse + aggregates for BI), orchestration with dependencies/retries, and the wrappers: data quality tests, observability/SLAs, governance, CI/CD — plus idempotency for safe reruns/backfills.

sd-worked-example.q: How do you handle late or corrected data in a batch pipeline?
sd-worked-example.a: Make loads idempotent (partition overwrite/upsert) so you can reprocess a day's partition when late/corrected data arrives; use updated_at and an overlap window to catch late rows; keep raw bronze so you can rebuild downstream; and use SCD2 for dimensions to preserve history. Backfills + idempotency correct data without duplicating it.

sd-storage-selection.q: How do you choose the right storage/database for a use case?
sd-storage-selection.a: Match the store to the access pattern: point reads/writes (OLTP) → relational DB; big scans/aggregations → warehouse/lakehouse; key lookups/cache → key-value (Redis/DynamoDB); full-text search → Elasticsearch; similarity/embeddings → vector DB; event streams → Kafka. There's no 'best' database — pick by how the data is written and read, plus scale, consistency, and cost.

sd-storage-selection.q: When key-value vs warehouse vs search engine?
sd-storage-selection.a: Key-value (Redis/DynamoDB) for fast lookups by a known key (sessions, features, cache) — O(1), no querying by value. Warehouse for analytical scans/aggregations over big data (columnar, SQL). Search engine (Elasticsearch) for full-text/relevance over text. Each optimizes a different access pattern; using the wrong one (a warehouse for high-QPS point lookups) performs poorly.

sd-storage-selection.q: Why is 'there is no best database' an important principle?
sd-storage-selection.a: Every store optimizes for specific access patterns and trade-offs (consistency, latency, scale, query type). A warehouse is great for analytics but terrible for high-QPS point lookups; a KV store is fast for lookups but can't do ad-hoc joins. Good design selects the right tool per workload (polyglot persistence) rather than forcing one database to do everything.

sd-scaling.q: Vertical vs horizontal scaling, and where does caching fit?
sd-scaling.a: Vertical (scale up) = a bigger machine — simple but capped and expensive. Horizontal (scale out) = more machines — near-limitless but needs distribution (partitioning, coordination). Caching scales reads by serving hot data from memory, offloading the backend. Data systems mostly scale out (Spark, Kafka, sharded DBs) and cache hot reads; vertical scaling is a stopgap with a ceiling.

sd-scaling.q: How do you scale the read path vs the write path?
sd-scaling.a: Reads: add replicas, caching (Redis/CDN), pre-computed aggregates/materialized views, read-optimized stores — spread and serve hot data fast. Writes: partition/shard by key (parallel writes), write-optimized storage (LSM), batch/buffer (Kafka), async processing. Reads scale via replication+caching; writes via partitioning+buffering. Find which path is the bottleneck first.

sd-scaling.q: What breaks first when a data system scales, and how do you address it?
sd-scaling.a: Usually a single bottleneck: a non-distributed DB hitting write limits (→ shard/partition), a hot partition/skew (→ better key/salting), the network/shuffle in distributed compute (→ minimize movement, broadcast), or a synchronous step that can't keep up (→ buffer with Kafka, process async). Measure to find it, then apply partitioning, caching, async, or a more scalable store.

sd-realtime-example.q: Design a real-time analytics / dashboard system.
sd-realtime-example.a: Events → Kafka (partitioned for parallelism/ordering) → stream processor (Flink/Spark SS) doing windowed aggregations with event-time + watermarks and exactly-once via checkpoints → write to a low-latency serving store (Redis/Druid/ClickHouse) that the dashboard queries. Add a schema registry, monitoring (consumer lag, watermarks), and a replay path (Kappa). Possibly keep a batch path for authoritative results (Lambda). Address scale (partitions), exactly-once, and late data.

sd-realtime-example.q: What serving store for a real-time dashboard, and why?
sd-realtime-example.a: A low-latency, query-optimized store the dashboard hits at high QPS: an OLAP store (Druid/ClickHouse/Pinot — fast aggregations on fresh data) or Redis for precomputed metrics. You don't query Kafka or a slow warehouse directly for sub-second dashboards. The stream processor computes aggregates and writes them to this fast serving layer, matched to the dashboard's latency/QPS.

sd-realtime-example.q: What are the hard parts of a real-time pipeline?
sd-realtime-example.a: Correctness under time (event-time windows, watermarks, late data), exactly-once across the path (idempotent sinks + checkpoints), state management/recovery, scaling/backpressure (partitions, consumer lag), schema evolution, and operability (monitoring, replay). The data flow is easy; correctness and reliability under failure and out-of-order data are the challenge.

sd-interview-process.q: How do you structure a system-design interview answer?
sd-interview-process.a: Clarify requirements first (scale, latency, consumers, constraints) — never assume. Then a high-level design (sketch ingest→store→transform→serve). Deep-dive key components and justify choices (storage, engine, batch vs stream). Address scale, cost, and trade-offs explicitly. Wrap up with failure modes, monitoring, and improvements. Throughout: think out loud, state assumptions, acknowledge trade-offs — there's no single right answer.

sd-interview-process.q: How do you answer behavioral questions in a DE interview?
sd-interview-process.a: Use STAR: Situation (context), Task (your goal), Action (what YOU specifically did), Result (outcome, quantified if possible). Pick stories showing ownership, handling ambiguity/failure, collaboration, and impact. Be specific and honest, focus on your contribution, and tie it to the role. Prepare a few flexible stories you can adapt to different prompts.

sd-interview-process.q: What are interviewers really evaluating in a system-design round?
sd-interview-process.a: Not a 'correct' answer, but your THINKING: do you gather requirements, structure the problem, make and justify reasonable choices, reason about scale/cost/trade-offs, handle failure/quality, and communicate clearly? They want to see how you'd approach a real ambiguous problem — pragmatism, breadth, and trade-off awareness — more than memorized architectures.

ml-de-role.q: What's the data engineer's role in ML?
ml-de-role.a: DEs build the data foundation ML runs on: reliable ingestion/labels, feature pipelines and feature stores, training-data prep (point-in-time correct, no leakage), serving infrastructure (batch/online), and the MLOps plumbing (monitoring, retraining triggers). The DE owns the data and pipelines; the DS owns the model. Most ML failure modes are data/pipeline problems — the DE's domain.

ml-de-role.q: Why is data engineering said to be most of ML in production?
ml-de-role.a: A production ML system is mostly data plumbing — ingestion, validation, feature computation, train/serve consistency, drift monitoring, retraining — with the model a small part. The hard, ongoing work (and most failures) is data quality, freshness, train/serve skew, and pipeline reliability, all DE work. 'ML is 90% data engineering' captures that the model is the easy 10%.

ml-de-role.q: What ML failure modes are really data-engineering problems?
ml-de-role.a: Train/serve skew (features computed differently in training vs serving), data leakage (future info in training), stale features, schema/distribution drift breaking the model silently, broken label pipelines, and non-reproducible training data. These are data/pipeline issues — which is why a feature store, point-in-time correctness, validation, and monitoring (all DE concerns) keep ML reliable.

ml-data.q: What is data leakage and how do you prevent it?
ml-data.a: Leakage is information unavailable at prediction time leaking into training data — inflating offline metrics but failing in production (e.g., a feature computed AFTER the event, or randomly splitting a time-series so the future leaks into train). Prevent it with point-in-time-correct features (only data known before the prediction), time-based splits for temporal data, and fitting transformers on train only. It's the #1 cause of 'great offline, terrible in prod'.

ml-data.q: How should you split data for training, and why does it matter?
ml-data.a: Train/validation/test to estimate generalization and tune without overfitting the test set. For TIME-SERIES, split by TIME (train on past, test on future) — never random — or you leak future info and overestimate performance. Stratify for imbalanced classes. The split must mimic how the model is used in production (predicting the future from the past).

ml-data.q: Why are labels often the hard part of ML data?
ml-data.a: Labels (the target) are frequently delayed (you learn if a user churned weeks later), expensive/manual, noisy, or biased. Building a reliable label pipeline — joining delayed outcomes back to predictions, handling label latency, ensuring quality — is core DE work and often the bottleneck. A model is only as good as its labels, and getting clean, timely labels at scale is hard.

ml-features.q: What is a feature store and what problem does it solve?
ml-features.a: A central system for defining, computing, storing, and serving ML features consistently to BOTH training (offline, historical, point-in-time correct) and serving (online, low-latency). It solves train/serve skew (one definition used everywhere), feature reuse across teams/models, and point-in-time correctness for training. It's the contract between data engineering and ML — the same feature computed identically for training and inference.

ml-features.q: What is train/serve skew and how does a feature store prevent it?
ml-features.a: Train/serve skew is when a feature is computed differently in training (batch, from history) than in serving (real-time, live) — so the model sees inconsistent inputs and underperforms in production. A feature store defines each feature ONCE and materializes it to both an offline store (training) and an online store (serving), guaranteeing identical computation. One definition, two consistent surfaces.

ml-features.q: What is point-in-time correctness for features?
ml-features.a: When building training data, each feature value must reflect ONLY what was known at the time of that historical event — not future data. A '30-day spend' feature for a Jan 1 label must use data up to Jan 1, not later; getting this wrong is leakage. Feature stores do point-in-time (as-of) joins to assemble historically-correct training sets — a key, tricky DE responsibility.

ml-training.q: What does a reproducible training pipeline need?
ml-training.a: Versioned data (or a point-in-time snapshot), versioned code, pinned dependencies/environment (container), fixed random seeds, and experiment tracking (MLflow) logging parameters, metrics, and artifacts — so a model can be reproduced and compared. Reproducibility lets you retrain the exact model, debug regressions, and audit; non-reproducible training is a production liability.

ml-training.q: What is experiment tracking and why use it?
ml-training.a: Logging each training run's parameters, metrics, code version, data version, and produced model artifact — so you can compare experiments, reproduce results, and objectively pick the best model. Tools like MLflow provide this. Without it you can't tell which run produced the deployed model or why one is better — essential for systematic, auditable ML.

ml-training.q: How does DE support model training at scale?
ml-training.a: By providing reliable, point-in-time-correct training data (from the feature store), scalable data prep (Spark for large datasets), reproducible pipelines (versioned, containerized), orchestration (scheduled retraining), and tracking infrastructure. The DE makes training data trustworthy and the pipeline reproducible and automatable; the DS focuses on architecture/tuning.

ml-registry.q: What is a model registry and why is it important?
ml-registry.a: A central store for trained models with versioning, stage transitions (Staging → Production → Archived), metadata (metrics, lineage to training data/code), and approval workflow. It answers 'which model is live, and how was it produced?' and decouples training from deployment — serving loads the current Production model without a redeploy. It's the contract between training and serving.

ml-registry.q: How does a registry enable safe model deployment?
ml-registry.a: It versions every model and tracks stages, so you can promote a validated model to Production, roll back to a previous version instantly if it misbehaves, run shadow/A-B tests between versions, and audit what's deployed. Serving references 'the Production model', so promotion is a metadata change, not a code deploy. It makes model releases controlled and reversible.

ml-registry.q: Why decouple the model registry from the serving code?
ml-registry.a: So you can update the model independently of the application — promote a new version in the registry and serving picks it up without redeploying code, and roll back by changing the stage. It separates the model lifecycle (train/validate/promote) from the app lifecycle, enabling frequent updates, fast rollback, and clear ownership. The registry is the integration point.

ml-serving.q: Batch vs online (real-time) inference — when each?
ml-serving.a: Batch inference precomputes predictions on a schedule for all entities and stores them (nightly churn scores → a table the app reads) — simple, cheap, high-throughput, but stale and only for known entities. Online inference computes on-demand via a low-latency API with real-time features — fresh, handles any input, but needs serving infra, low-latency feature lookup, and scaling. Use batch when slightly-stale precomputed scores suffice; online when you need fresh, on-demand predictions.

ml-serving.q: What does online model serving infrastructure need?
ml-serving.a: A low-latency prediction service (API), an online feature store for fast feature lookup at request time (same features as training — no skew), the model loaded from the registry (Production version), autoscaling for QPS, monitoring (latency, throughput, prediction logging), and fallback handling. The hard parts are low-latency feature retrieval and consistency with training features.

ml-serving.q: How do you log predictions and why?
ml-serving.a: Log each prediction with its input features, model version, timestamp, and an ID — so you can later JOIN delayed actual outcomes (labels) to evaluate real-world performance, detect drift, debug, and audit. Without prediction logging you can't measure how the model actually performs in production or compute the metrics that trigger retraining. It closes the monitoring loop.

ml-mlops.q: What is MLOps and how does it differ from DevOps?
ml-mlops.a: MLOps applies DevOps practices (CI/CD, automation, monitoring) to ML, plus ML-specific concerns: you version and test DATA and MODELS (not just code), monitor for DATA/CONCEPT DRIFT (the world changes, degrading the model), and automate RETRAINING (continuous training) when performance decays. The added dimensions are data, models, and drift — a model can rot even with frozen code because the input distribution shifts.

ml-mlops.q: What is model drift and how do you detect it?
ml-mlops.a: Drift is performance degrading over time because data changes: DATA drift (input distributions shift) or CONCEPT drift (the feature-target relationship changes). Detect it by monitoring feature distributions vs a training reference (e.g., Evidently) and tracking live performance via delayed labels (actual outcomes vs predictions). When drift/performance crosses a threshold, trigger retraining.

ml-mlops.q: How do you close the loop with automated retraining?
ml-mlops.a: Monitor drift and live performance (logged predictions + delayed labels); when they breach a threshold (or on schedule), automatically trigger the training pipeline → register the new model → validate (shadow/A-B) → promote to Production if better → serving picks it up. This 'continuous training' loop keeps the model fresh as the world changes — orchestrated like any pipeline, which is why it's DE/MLOps work.

ml-vectors.q: What are embeddings and vector databases, and a DE use case?
ml-vectors.a: Embeddings are dense vectors representing data (text, images) by semantic meaning, produced by ML models. A vector database stores them and does approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search to find semantically similar items. DE use cases: semantic search, recommendations, and RAG for LLMs — the DE builds the pipeline that generates embeddings, loads the vector DB, and serves similarity queries.

ml-vectors.q: What is RAG and the data engineer's role in it?
ml-vectors.a: Retrieval-Augmented Generation grounds an LLM's answers in your data: embed your documents into a vector DB, and at query time retrieve the most relevant chunks (ANN) to feed the LLM as context. The DE builds the ingestion/chunking/embedding pipeline, manages the vector store (freshness, updates), and serves retrieval — a data pipeline problem (chunk, embed, index, retrieve), squarely DE work.

ml-vectors.q: What is ANN and why approximate?
ml-vectors.a: Approximate Nearest Neighbor search finds the vectors closest to a query without scanning them all — exact search over millions/billions of high-dimensional vectors is too slow. ANN (HNSW, IVF) trades a tiny bit of recall for massive speed via clever index structures. It's what makes vector search and semantic retrieval practical at scale.

cap-api-to-lake.q: Walk me through your API-to-lake ingestion project.
cap-api-to-lake.a: A pipeline ingesting from a paginated REST API into a queryable lake: handle pagination + rate limits + retries with backoff, checkpoint progress for resumability, land raw JSON in bronze (immutable), validate + flatten to typed Parquet in silver, and query with DuckDB/Trino — idempotent (upsert by key) so reruns are safe. I'd highlight the resilience (retries/checkpointing), the raw-then-modeled layering, and why Parquet for the queryable layer.

cap-api-to-lake.q: What were the reliability challenges ingesting from an API?
cap-api-to-lake.a: APIs fail and rate-limit, so: exponential backoff + jitter on transient errors, respect 429/Retry-After, set timeouts, paginate via cursor, and CHECKPOINT the last successful cursor so a failed run resumes instead of restarting. Idempotency (upsert by key) means retries don't duplicate, and the raw bronze layer lets me reprocess if parsing changes. Resilience and idempotency are the core lessons.

cap-api-to-lake.q: How would you scale this ingestion pipeline?
cap-api-to-lake.a: Parallelize across API partitions/endpoints (respecting rate limits), incremental loads via a high-watermark (only new data), partition the lake by date for pruning, and orchestrate with retries/alerting. If volume grows huge, move parsing to Spark and the query layer to a warehouse/lakehouse. The design (raw bronze + incremental + idempotent) already supports scaling out.

cap-files-to-report.a: A pipeline that ingests messy files (CSV/Excel), validates and cleans them (schema, types, dedup, nulls), models the data, computes metrics, and publishes a report/dashboard. Defensive parsing (explicit dtypes, bad rows → quarantine), data quality tests before publishing, and idempotent output. I'd highlight the validation/quarantine (don't publish bad data) and turning raw files into a trustworthy published artifact.

cap-files-to-report.q: How did you handle messy/inconsistent input files?
cap-files-to-report.a: Defensively: explicit schema/dtypes on read, handle encoding (UTF-8), validate (required columns, types, ranges), route bad rows to quarantine instead of failing the load, dedup, and normalize. Then data quality tests gate publishing. Excel/CSV are fragile (type ambiguity, merged cells, manual edits), so converting to clean typed Parquet early and validating is key.

cap-files-to-report.q: How do you ensure the published report is trustworthy?
cap-files-to-report.a: Data quality tests (schema, not-null, ranges, reconciliation to source totals) run before publishing; on failure, don't publish (circuit-break) and alert. Idempotent generation so reruns are consistent, plus lineage from report back to source. The principle: a report is only as trustworthy as the gated, validated pipeline behind it — never publish unvalidated data.

cap-orchestrated.a: A multi-step pipeline orchestrated (Prefect/Airflow) as a DAG with dependencies, retries, and scheduling; each step idempotent; data quality checks gate progression (bad data quarantined, critical failures circuit-break); plus monitoring/alerting. I'd discuss why orchestration (dependencies, retries, backfills, visibility) over cron, and how quality gates stop bad data propagating.

cap-orchestrated.q: Why use an orchestrator instead of cron for this?
cap-orchestrated.a: Cron only schedules; the orchestrator gives dependency management (run transform only after extract succeeds), retries, backfills, parameterized runs, state, logging, alerting, and a UI to see what ran and why it failed. For a multi-step quality-gated pipeline those are essential for reliability and operability — cron would leave me blind and brittle.

cap-orchestrated.q: How did you make the pipeline idempotent and recoverable?
cap-orchestrated.a: Each task overwrites its partition or upserts by key (no blind appends), parameterized by the run date (no leaking now()), with retries + backoff. So a retried or backfilled run produces the same result and I can reprocess any date safely. Combined with the orchestrator's retry/backfill and quality gates, it recovers cleanly from failures.

cap-benchmark.q: Describe your synthetic-data engine benchmark project.
cap-benchmark.a: I generated realistic synthetic data (Faker) at scale and benchmarked engines (pandas vs Polars vs DuckDB vs Spark) on the same workloads, measuring runtime and memory — reproducible (seeded data, pinned envs). I'd discuss the findings: pandas hits a memory/speed wall; Polars/DuckDB are far faster on a single node via lazy execution + pushdown; Spark wins at distributed scale — match the engine to the data size.

cap-benchmark.q: What did benchmarking the engines teach you?
cap-benchmark.a: The right engine depends on data size and workload: pandas is fine for small in-memory data but slow/memory-bound; Polars and DuckDB are dramatically faster on a single machine (multi-core, lazy, larger-than-RAM via streaming + pushdown); Spark is for distributed scale but has overhead. The lesson: don't default to pandas/Spark — DuckDB/Polars cover a huge middle ground efficiently.

cap-benchmark.q: How did you make the benchmark fair and reproducible?
cap-benchmark.a: Same data (seeded synthetic generation), same queries, same hardware, warm-up runs, multiple trials, measuring both time and peak memory; pinned library versions and a containerized environment. Reproducibility matters so the comparison is trustworthy and re-runnable — otherwise benchmark numbers are meaningless.

cap-streaming-pipeline.q: Walk me through your real-time streaming pipeline.
cap-streaming-pipeline.a: Events → Kafka (partitioned) → stream processor (Spark Structured Streaming / Flink) doing windowed aggregations with event-time + watermarks and exactly-once via checkpointing → serving store (or lakehouse) for low-latency reads. Handles late data, schema via a registry, and consumer-lag monitoring. I'd discuss event-time correctness, exactly-once, and how the replayable Kafka log lets me reprocess.

cap-streaming-pipeline.q: What were the hard parts of the streaming capstone?
cap-streaming-pipeline.a: Correctness under time (event-time windows + watermarks for late/out-of-order data), exactly-once (idempotent sink + checkpointing), bounding state, and monitoring consumer lag. The data flow is straightforward; getting correct results despite late, out-of-order events and failures is the real challenge — which is why watermarks, checkpoints, and idempotency are central.

cap-streaming-pipeline.q: How does your streaming pipeline handle failures and reprocessing?
cap-streaming-pipeline.a: Checkpointing state + offsets to durable storage means on failure it restores the last consistent snapshot and resumes (exactly-once). Because Kafka is a replayable log, I can reset offsets and replay history through corrected code to reprocess/backfill (the Kappa idea), writing to a new output version. Durability + idempotency make it fault-tolerant and fixable.

cap-dbt-warehouse.a: A dbt project transforming raw warehouse data into a tested, documented dimensional model: staging → intermediate → marts (fact + dims), using ref() for the dependency DAG, dbt tests (not-null, unique, relationships) for quality, and generated docs + lineage — version-controlled with CI. I'd discuss how dbt brings software engineering (modularity, testing, CI, lineage) to analytics SQL ('analytics engineering').

cap-dbt-warehouse.q: How does dbt improve over hand-written SQL transformations?
cap-dbt-warehouse.a: dbt adds dependency management (ref() builds the DAG and run order), testing (declarative data tests in CI and on build), documentation + lineage (auto-generated), modularity (reusable models/macros), and environment portability. It turns ad-hoc SQL scripts into a version-controlled, tested, documented transformation codebase — reliability and maintainability raw SQL lacks.

cap-dbt-warehouse.q: How did you ensure data quality in the dbt project?
cap-dbt-warehouse.a: dbt tests declared next to models — not-null, unique, accepted_values, relationships, plus custom SQL tests — run in CI (on PRs) and on every build, failing the run if violated so bad data doesn't propagate. Combined with staging-layer cleaning and docs, quality is enforced as code, reviewed and versioned — turning quality into automated, gated assertions.

cap-lakehouse-medallion.a: Bronze (raw, immutable), silver (cleaned, validated, conformed, deduplicated), and gold (business aggregates) layers on object storage using a table format (Delta/Iceberg) for ACID + time travel + schema enforcement, with MERGE for upserts/CDC and OPTIMIZE for compaction. I'd discuss why the table format (reliability on cheap storage), the layered quality progression, and reprocessing downstream from bronze.

cap-lakehouse-medallion.q: Why use a table format instead of plain Parquet?
cap-lakehouse-medallion.a: Plain Parquet is just files with no transactional guarantees. The table format adds ACID (consistent concurrent reads/writes), schema enforcement/evolution, time travel, and efficient MERGE/upsert/delete — turning a pile of Parquet into a reliable table. That's what makes the lakehouse (warehouse reliability on cheap lake storage) possible, and enables CDC apply and GDPR deletes.

cap-lakehouse-medallion.q: How does the medallion structure help operability?
cap-lakehouse-medallion.a: Each layer has a clear contract (bronze=raw, silver=clean/granular, gold=aggregated/served), so quality improves progressively and is testable at each boundary. Because bronze is immutable raw, I can reprocess silver/gold from it after a bug or logic change without re-extracting from the source. It gives auditability, clear ownership, and safe reprocessing.

cap-cdc-sync.a: I captured changes from an operational database via CDC (reading the transaction log) and synced them into the lakehouse, applying inserts/updates/deletes with MERGE into a target table — keeping the lake in sync near-real-time with low source load, including deletes (which naive extracts miss) and exactly-once. I'd discuss why log-based CDC (captures deletes, low impact) and how MERGE + ACID make applying changes reliable.

cap-cdc-sync.q: Why is log-based CDC better than periodically extracting changed rows?
cap-cdc-sync.a: Log-based CDC reads the DB's write-ahead/binlog — capturing EVERY change including hard deletes, with low source impact (reads the log, not tables), near-real-time. Query polling ('WHERE updated_at > last_run') misses hard deletes, adds source query load, and can miss changes between polls. CDC is more accurate and efficient for syncing operational data.

cap-cdc-sync.q: How do you apply CDC changes to a lake table reliably?
cap-cdc-sync.a: MERGE the CDC stream into the target by key — update changed rows, insert new ones, delete tombstoned ones — atomically, using a table format (Delta/Iceberg) for ACID. Handle ordering (apply in commit order) and idempotency (re-applying the same change is safe). The table format's MERGE + ACID is what makes applying updates/deletes (impossible on immutable raw files) reliable.

cap-ml-pipeline.q: Walk me through your feature-store-to-served-model project.
cap-ml-pipeline.a: An end-to-end ML system: ingest reliable data → compute point-in-time-correct features into a feature store (offline + online) → train with experiment tracking (MLflow) → register and promote the model → serve batch + online (consistent online features, no skew) → monitor for drift → retrain when it decays. The two contracts are the feature store (train/serve consistency) and the registry (which model is live). I'd discuss train/serve skew prevention and the monitoring/retraining loop.

cap-ml-pipeline.q: What are the key 'seams' in a production ML system, and how does your design hold them?
cap-ml-pipeline.a: training↔serving (skew → held by the feature store, one definition); training↔deployment ('which model is live?' → held by the registry, Production stage); deploy↔reality (silent decay → held by monitoring + retraining); any rerun (duplication → idempotent loads); measuring truth (late labels → a delayed-label join). My design puts a concrete mechanism at each seam — that's what makes the ML system reliable.

cap-ml-pipeline.q: How does the data engineer's work make this ML system reliable?
cap-ml-pipeline.a: By owning the data contracts: a feature store guaranteeing identical features for training and serving (no skew, point-in-time correct), reliable validated ingestion and labels, reproducible pipelines, a registry decoupling model from serving, and monitoring + automated retraining. Most ML production failures are data/pipeline failures — the DE engineering these seams keeps the model working over time.

sd-recommendation.q: Design an event-tracking and recommendation pipeline.
sd-recommendation.a: Clients emit interaction events (views/clicks/purchases) → ingest to Kafka (partitioned by user) → both stream and batch paths: compute interaction features (point-in-time correct) into a feature store, train a recommendation model (collaborative filtering / embeddings), and serve recommendations — precomputed batch recs in a fast store (Redis) and/or online ranking from a registry model with online features. Add monitoring (drift, CTR) and a replay path. Discuss event-time correctness, train/serve consistency, and scaling via partitions.

sd-recommendation.q: How do you serve recommendations at low latency?
sd-recommendation.a: Often a two-stage design: cheap candidate generation (precomputed nightly into a key-value store keyed by user, or ANN over item embeddings in a vector DB) + a lightweight online ranking model served from a registry using online features. Precomputed candidates give instant lookup; online ranking adds freshness/personalization. Match the serving design to the latency and freshness the use case needs.

sd-recommendation.q: How does event tracking feed recommendations?
sd-recommendation.a: User interaction events (implicit feedback — views, clicks, dwell, purchases) are both the training signal (labels = engagement) and the features for recommendations. The pipeline ingests events reliably (Kafka), computes interaction features point-in-time-correctly into a feature store, and uses them to train the model and serve fresh user context. Reliable, low-skew event data is what makes recommendations work — squarely DE work.

ml-fundamentals.a: Supervised learns from labeled examples (input X → known output y) to predict — classification (category) or regression (number); it needs labels. Unsupervised finds structure in unlabeled data — clustering (group) or dimensionality reduction. Supervised is the bulk of industry ML; the label requirement is what makes it a data-engineering problem (sourcing/pipelining labels).

Both supervised. Classification predicts a discrete category (spam/not-spam, churn/stay); regression predicts a continuous number (price, demand). The distinction drives the model, the loss, and crucially the evaluation metric (precision/recall/F1/AUC vs RMSE/MAE/R²).

ml-fundamentals.q: Why is ML said to be 'mostly data engineering'?
ml-fundamentals.a: The model is a small part; the bulk is data plumbing — reliable data + labels, EDA, preprocessing, features (train/serve consistency), training pipelines, serving, and drift monitoring. The hard, ongoing work and most production failures are data/pipeline problems, which is DE work. The DE owns the whole lifecycle except the model architecture and tuning.

ml-eda.q: What is EDA and why does a data engineer do it?
ml-eda.a: Exploratory Data Analysis is profiling a dataset before modeling — shape/types, missing values, distributions, outliers, correlations, target balance — to understand its structure, quality, and relationships. A DE does it as data profiling: to validate pipelines (did we drop rows? is a column mis-typed or mostly null?), catch quality issues, and inform cleaning and features. It's the same profiling that powers data-quality monitoring.

ml-eda.q: What would you check in EDA, and what does each decision inform?
ml-eda.a: Shape/types (fix mis-typed columns, sizing), missing values (impute vs drop), distributions (scaling/transforms, encoding by cardinality), outliers (clean vs cap), correlations (drop redundant/leaky features), and target balance (metric choice — accuracy lies on imbalanced data). Each finding drives a concrete pipeline or modeling decision.

ml-eda.q: How do you do EDA on data too big to fit in memory?
ml-eda.a: Don't load it all — compute the same statistics as aggregations in DuckDB/Spark/SQL over the full data or a representative sample (counts, null rates, min/max/avg, cardinality, distributions), and use automated profilers (ydata-profiling) on a sample for a fast overview. A DE does EDA at scale with set-based aggregation, not by pulling everything into pandas.

ml-preprocessing.q: What are the core preprocessing steps, and which models need scaling?
ml-preprocessing.a: Impute missing (median/mode), encode categoricals (one-hot for low cardinality, target/frequency for high, ordinal for ordered), and scale numerics (standardize/min-max). Scaling is needed for linear, distance, and gradient-based models (logistic regression, SVM, neural nets, k-means); tree-based models (trees, forests, gradient boosting) need no scaling because they split on thresholds.

ml-preprocessing.q: What is data leakage in preprocessing, and how do you prevent it?
ml-preprocessing.a: Leakage is when test-set information influences training — e.g., fitting a scaler's mean/std or an imputer's median over ALL data before splitting, so test statistics leak into training and inflate offline metrics. Prevent it by fitting transformers on the TRAINING set only, then applying to validation/test (and serving). Wrap it in a pipeline so the fit-on-train discipline is automatic and the same fitted transforms apply everywhere.

ml-preprocessing.q: How does preprocessing relate to train/serve skew?
ml-preprocessing.a: The fitted transformations (imputation values, encodings, scaling stats) must run identically in training (batch, from history) and serving (real-time). If they differ, the model sees inconsistent inputs and underperforms — train/serve skew, the #1 ML-in-prod failure. A serialized pipeline or a feature store applies one fitted transformation in both places, preventing skew (and leakage).

ml-algorithms.q: What's the go-to algorithm for tabular data, and why should a DE care?
ml-algorithms.a: Gradient boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM) — the accurate, low-preprocessing default for structured enterprise data. A DE cares because the algorithm dictates the infrastructure: boosting needs clean tabular features and CPU; deep learning needs huge labeled data, GPUs, and embedding pipelines; k-means needs scaled numerics. Knowing the algorithm lets you size compute and design the right feature/serving pipeline.

ml-algorithms.q: Which model families need feature scaling and which don't?
ml-algorithms.a: Tree-based models (decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting) need NO scaling — they split on thresholds, so scale is irrelevant, and they handle categoricals/missing gracefully. Linear, distance, and gradient models (logistic regression, SVM, k-means, neural nets) DO need scaled, fully-numeric inputs. This determines your preprocessing pipeline.

ml-algorithms.q: How does choosing deep learning vs gradient boosting change the data engineering?
ml-algorithms.a: Deep learning (images/text/huge data) needs large labeled datasets, GPU infrastructure, embedding/vector pipelines, and heavier (often batched) serving. Gradient boosting (tabular) needs clean tabular features and CPU, with fast low-latency serving. So the model family directly changes what you provision — GPUs and embedding stores vs tabular feature pipelines.

ml-evaluation.q: Why is accuracy a poor metric on imbalanced data, and what do you use instead?
ml-evaluation.a: With heavy imbalance (99% one class), a model that always predicts the majority class is ~99% accurate while being useless — accuracy rewards ignoring the minority. Use precision (cost of false positives), recall (cost of false negatives), F1 (their balance), and ROC/PR-AUC. Pick the metric for the business cost — e.g., maximize recall for fraud so you don't miss cases.

ml-evaluation.q: Explain train/validation/test and why you only touch test once.
ml-evaluation.a: Train fits the model; validation tunes hyperparameters and selects models (used repeatedly); test gives a final, one-time unbiased estimate of generalization. If you tune on the test set it stops being unbiased — you've effectively trained on it, so it no longer predicts real-world performance. For time-series, split by time, not randomly, to avoid leaking the future.

ml-evaluation.q: How do evaluation metrics relate to production monitoring?
ml-evaluation.a: Production monitoring IS evaluation on live data: you log each prediction, join the delayed actual labels when they arrive, and compute the metric (recall, F1, RMSE — whichever matters) over time; when it drops below the SLO, you trigger retraining. So you must know which metric is right and how to compute it from logged predictions + labels. Evaluation is the foundation of the drift/performance monitoring loop the DE builds.

cloud-fundamentals.q: Why did data teams move to the cloud?
cloud-fundamentals.a: Elasticity (scale to spiky data workloads and back, paying only for the burst), pay-per-use (no idle CapEx), managed services (build pipelines instead of running servers), separation of storage and compute (store cheaply, compute on demand — the lakehouse), global reach + durability, and speed (provision in minutes). The economics fit data's spiky, scale-out nature far better than fixed on-prem hardware.

cloud-fundamentals.q: Explain public vs private vs hybrid vs multi-cloud.
cloud-fundamentals.a: Public = shared provider infrastructure (AWS/GCP/Azure — the default, max elasticity). Private = dedicated infra (on-prem or hosted) for control/compliance. Hybrid = public + private/on-prem connected (common during migration or for data residency). Multi-cloud = multiple public providers for resilience / avoiding lock-in / best-of-breed — but adds complexity and cross-cloud egress cost.

cloud-fundamentals.q: What is the single biggest cloud-native advantage for data platforms?
cloud-fundamentals.a: Separation of storage and compute — store data cheaply once on object storage and spin compute up only when querying, scaling and paying for each independently. It enables elastic pay-per-use analytics, workload isolation (multiple engines on the same data), and is the foundation of the lakehouse. On-prem couples storage to compute, which is rigid and wasteful.

cloud-service-models.a: They differ by how much of the stack you manage vs the provider. IaaS = raw VMs (you manage OS, runtime, app; provider manages hardware/network/virtualization — EC2). PaaS = managed runtime (you manage app + data; provider also handles OS, scaling, patching — RDS, BigQuery). SaaS = ready-made apps you just use (Gmail, Salesforce, dbt Cloud). More managed left to right: more convenience, less control.

cloud-service-models.q: Where do most data-engineering workloads sit on the service-model spectrum, and why?
cloud-service-models.a: Mostly PaaS and serverless (managed warehouses like BigQuery/Snowflake, managed Spark, managed orchestration) plus SaaS tools (Fivetran, dbt Cloud, Looker). Because operational toil — patching, scaling, running servers — is the bottleneck, not raw control; managed services let the team build pipelines and models. IaaS is reserved for bespoke needs a managed service can't meet.

cloud-service-models.q: What is serverless, and how does it relate to PaaS?
cloud-service-models.a: Serverless is a refinement of PaaS where you deploy code or queries with NO server management — the platform provisions and scales automatically, and you pay per invocation/byte (Lambda, BigQuery on-demand, Cloud Run). It's the maximum-convenience end of the spectrum, ideal for spiky/event-driven workloads; the trade-off is less control and watching cost/cold-starts.

cloud-architecture.q: Explain regions and availability zones, and how you'd use them for a reliable pipeline.
cloud-architecture.a: A region is a geographic area (us-east-1); an AZ is an isolated data center within it (independent power/cooling/network), and a region has several. For reliability, deploy across multiple AZs — replicate storage and run clusters/managed DBs multi-AZ — so a single data-center failure doesn't take you down. Choose the region for latency, data residency, and cost; go multi-region for disaster recovery (higher cost + egress).

cloud-architecture.q: Explain the shared responsibility model with a data example.
cloud-architecture.a: Security is split: the provider secures OF the cloud (physical hardware, network, hypervisor, managed-service internals); you secure IN the cloud (your data, IAM/access, configuration, encryption, apps). Example: AWS keeps S3's infrastructure secure, but if YOU make a bucket public or grant over-broad IAM, that's your responsibility — and most cloud breaches are exactly these customer-side misconfigurations. The line shifts by service model (SaaS covers more).

cloud-architecture.q: What are the well-architected pillars, and which matter most for a data platform?
cloud-architecture.a: Reliability, Security, Cost optimization, Performance efficiency, Operational excellence (+ Sustainability). All matter, but for data platforms cost optimization (data is huge; egress and idle compute add up) and reliability (pipelines feed decisions) are front-of-mind, with security non-negotiable for regulated data. The point is to weigh them as explicit trade-offs in every design.

cap-rag-ingestion.q: You're asked to build the ingestion pipeline for a company-wide RAG assistant. Walk through the stages and the one decision that most affects answer quality.
cap-rag-ingestion.a: Stages: load & parse (text + metadata: source_uri, acl) → chunk → embed (after PII redaction) → upsert to a vector store keyed by stable chunk_id; online: query → embed → hybrid search → rerank → LLM. The decision that most affects quality is chunking: recursive ~512-token chunks with 10-20% overlap is the 2026 default (256-512 for factoid, 512-1024 for multi-hop). Wrong bracket costs 15-30% retrieval precision. Add metadata enrichment, which lifts accuracy more than swapping embedding models.

cap-rag-ingestion.q: How do you make re-ingestion idempotent and keep answers auditable?
cap-rag-ingestion.a: Give every chunk a stable, deterministic chunk_id (e.g. doc_id:index) and upsert by it, so a re-run updates in place instead of duplicating. Store source_uri (and version/timestamp) in the payload so every answer can cite where it came from and you can update/delete specific content deterministically. Keep the raw source for replay.

cap-rag-ingestion.q: This RAG system serves sensitive internal documents. How do you ensure a user can only get answers from documents they're authorized to see, and that PII never leaks via the index?
cap-rag-ingestion.a: Two controls. (1) Retrieval-native access control: attach access tags (acl) to each chunk's metadata and filter the vector search by the requesting user's permissions, so unauthorized chunks are never retrieved — don't rely on the LLM to self-censor. (2) Redact/tokenize PII before embedding, because embeddings bake the original text into vectors that are hard to scrub afterward; combine PII detection with schema validation at ingest. Add audit logging of which source_uris fed each answer.

cap-iceberg-lakehouse.q: Why has the industry converged on Apache Iceberg + a REST catalog, and what does the catalog give you that files alone don't?
cap-iceberg-lakehouse.a: Iceberg adds a metadata layer over Parquet giving ACID, time travel, hidden partitioning and schema evolution. The REST catalog is the single source of truth with server-side compare-and-swap, which is what makes concurrent writes on object storage safe. Because every major engine speaks the REST protocol, you get a genuinely multi-engine lakehouse over one copy of data (Spark ETL, Flink streaming, Trino/Dremio queries, DuckDB) with no lock-in — swap catalog implementations without changing engine config.

cap-iceberg-lakehouse.q: Your Flink job streams into an Iceberg table and queries have gotten slow over weeks. Diagnose and fix.
cap-iceberg-lakehouse.a: Streaming ingest writes many small files and accumulates snapshots/manifests, hurting read planning and data skipping. Fix with routine maintenance: compaction (rewrite_data_files) to merge small files, expire_snapshots to bound metadata/storage growth, rewrite_manifests for manifest hygiene, and refresh column stats so engines skip files. Tune commit/target-file-size on the writer to produce fewer, larger files up front.

cap-iceberg-lakehouse.q: When would you choose Delta Lake over Iceberg, and vice-versa?
cap-iceberg-lakehouse.a: Both give ACID over object storage. Choose Delta when you're Databricks-centric and want its tight platform integration (Unity Catalog, optimized runtime). Choose Iceberg for an open, multi-engine, no-lock-in lakehouse coordinated by a REST catalog (Polaris/Gravitino/cloud), where Spark, Trino, Flink and DuckDB all read one copy. The fundamentals (snapshots, MERGE, time travel) are similar; the decision is ecosystem and openness.

cap-data-contracts.q: What is a data contract, and what makes one actually prevent incidents rather than just document intent?
cap-data-contracts.a: A data contract is a machine-readable agreement between producer and consumers pinning schema/types, semantics, SLAs (freshness, volume, null rates) and owner — written in ODCS YAML and kept in the repo. It prevents incidents only when enforced in CI: datacontract-cli (ODCS) or buf (Protobuf) validates a proposed change against the contract and fails the build, so a breaking change is blocked at the PR instead of breaking dashboards in production. Documentation without an enforced CI gate is decorative.

cap-data-contracts.q: Explain 'shift-left' for data quality and the building blocks you'd put in place.
cap-data-contracts.a: Shift-left means catching data issues at the producer/commit/ingest stage rather than after downstream breakage. Three blocks: (1) contracts — define 'good' and ownership (ODCS YAML in repo); (2) quality gates — enforce expectations where data is created/transformed (dbt tests, Great Expectations, Soda at runtime; datacontract-cli/buf at registration time in CI); (3) feedback loops — detect, triage, trace impact via lineage, improve. Start with YAML + CI checks; migrate to a platform later since definitions transfer.

cap-data-contracts.q: A producer team frequently ships schema changes that silently break downstream regulatory reports. Design the guardrail without blocking their velocity unreasonably.
cap-data-contracts.a: Put the report's requirements into an ODCS contract (required columns, types, null rates, freshness SLA, owner) in the repo. Add a CI gate (datacontract-cli) on the producer's pipeline that diffs the proposed schema against the contract: non-breaking/additive changes pass automatically; breaking changes (drop/retype/nullable on required fields) fail the build with a clear message and require an explicit, reviewed contract version bump that notifies consumers. Pair with runtime checks (dbt tests/Soda) and lineage so impact is visible. Velocity stays high for safe changes; only breaking ones are gated.

cap-realtime-analytics.q: A product team wants an in-app dashboard updating within a second under heavy concurrent load. Why won't your batch warehouse do, and what do you build?
cap-realtime-analytics.a: Batch warehouses are optimized for large scans on yesterday's data, not sub-second, high-QPS serving on fresh data. Build a real-time OLAP path: event sources → Kafka → (Flink to enrich/aggregate) → a real-time OLAP store (ClickHouse / Pinot / Druid) → the dashboard. Speed comes from columnar storage + pre-aggregation + indexes, giving ms latency with ingestion-to-query under a second.

cap-realtime-analytics.q: Compare ClickHouse, Pinot and Druid for user-facing analytics and justify a choice for single-digit-ms latency at very high QPS.
cap-realtime-analytics.a: For single-digit-ms at very high concurrency on user-facing analytics, choose Pinot (or Druid): star-tree indexing + pre-aggregation are purpose-built for ultra-low latency at high QPS, accepting a multi-component ops cost (Controller/Broker/Server + ZooKeeper). ClickHouse is a single binary — simplest ops, excellent general OLAP, can consolidate workloads to cut cost — but its streaming ingest is micro-batch, so it's the better pick when query patterns are flexible and ops simplicity/cost matter more than guaranteed sub-second freshness at extreme QPS.

cap-realtime-analytics.q: What concrete techniques keep real-time OLAP queries fast as data grows?
cap-realtime-analytics.a: Pre-aggregate the metrics you serve; index the dimensions you filter on (e.g. Pinot star-tree); keep the served table narrow; set TTLs so the live table stays hot and small; partition by time; and push heavy historical analysis to the batch warehouse, keeping the OLAP store for the live window. The principle is the same as warehouse tuning — scan/aggregate the least data possible.

cap-finops-observability.q: What are the pillars of data observability, and how do they help you respond to an incident?
cap-finops-observability.a: Five pillars: freshness (on time?), volume (row-count anomalies?), schema (unexpected drift?), distribution/quality (values in range, null rates), and lineage (upstream/downstream map). On an incident you alert against an SLO when a pillar breaks, then use lineage to trace root cause upstream and blast radius downstream — turning a vague 'numbers look wrong' into a targeted fix. Implement with dbt tests/Great Expectations/Soda plus a catalog for lineage.

cap-finops-observability.q: Leadership says the cloud data bill doubled. As the lead, how do you investigate and bring it down without harming reliability?
cap-finops-observability.a: Treat cost as an observable metric. Tag resources and attribute spend per pipeline/team (query warehouse usage by tag) to find the top offenders. Typical culprits: idle/oversized warehouses, unpruned full-table scans, cross-region egress, tiny-file compaction overhead, runaway streaming. Act: auto-suspend idle compute, right-size clusters, partition/cluster so queries prune, fix data locality to cut egress, and kill zombie jobs — each measured against unchanged freshness/quality SLOs so reliability is preserved. Then dashboard cost with owners and SLOs so it stays controlled.

cap-finops-observability.q: Why pair data observability with FinOps, and how do they relate to data contracts?
cap-finops-observability.a: They answer the two questions leadership always asks: 'can I trust this number?' (observability) and 'why is the bill so high?' (FinOps). Together they make a platform operable — trustworthy and affordable. They relate to contracts as the runtime + cost half of quality: contracts enforce schema/SLA at the source (shift-left, in CI), while observability monitors freshness/volume/schema/quality/lineage at runtime and FinOps monitors spend. Contracts prevent breakage; observability catches what slips through; FinOps keeps it sustainable.

ib-overview.q: Walk me through what you expect in our data engineer interview loop.
