ib-overview.a: Recruiter screen → 1-2 technical phone screens → an onsite of 4-5 rounds (45-60 min): coding/DSA, SQL, data modeling, system/pipeline design, and a behavioral round (Googleyness & leadership). I'd expect clean coding with clear communication, window-function SQL, a modeling/design discussion with trade-offs, and BigQuery/GCP fluency to help.

ib-overview.q: How would you prepare differently for our loop versus a typical one?
ib-overview.a: Amazon embeds Leadership Principles in every round, including coding, so I'd bring 12-15 quantified STAR stories tagged to multiple principles (Ownership, Dive Deep, Deliver Results, Customer Obsession), and expect a Bar Raiser. Technically I'd still drill SQL, DSA, data modeling and pipeline design, but I'd consciously weave an LP signal into each technical answer.

ib-overview.q: Which round do you think matters most here, and why?
ib-overview.a: For Meta DE, data modeling carries the most weight and is usually introduced through a product-sense prompt ('model the data for this feature'). I'd lead with clarifying the product questions, name the fact grain, design dims with SCD2 where history matters, and tie it to measurable impact — while still being solid on SQL and Python.

ib-sql.q: Find the second-highest salary in an employees table.
ib-sql.a: SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp); — or generalize to Nth with DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) and filter = N (DENSE_RANK handles ties cleanly).

ib-sql.q: Return the top 3 products by sales within each category.
ib-sql.a: Use a window: SELECT * FROM (SELECT category, product, sales, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) rn FROM products) t WHERE rn<=3. ROW_NUMBER for strict top-3; switch to RANK/DENSE_RANK if ties should be included. This top-N-per-group pattern is the most common SQL-round ask.

ib-sql.q: From an events table, assign a session id where a new session starts after 30 minutes of inactivity per user.
ib-sql.a: Gaps-and-islands: flag a new session with CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user ORDER BY ts) > INTERVAL '30' MINUTE OR LAG(...) IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END, then session_id = SUM(flag) OVER (PARTITION BY user ORDER BY ts). The cumulative sum of break-flags forms contiguous session groups.

WITH m AS (SELECT date_trunc('month',ts) mo, SUM(amount) rev FROM orders GROUP BY 1) SELECT mo, rev, 100.0*(rev-LAG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mo))/LAG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mo) AS growth_pct FROM m ORDER BY mo. LAG() gives the prior period; guard divide-by-zero/NULL for the first month.

ib-sql.q: This query is slow on a large table — how do you optimize it?
ib-sql.a: Clarify the plan: ensure filters hit indexed/partition columns (partition pruning), avoid SELECT * (projection), push predicates before joins, verify join keys are unique on one side to avoid fan-out, pre-aggregate or materialize hot rollups, and in a per-byte-scanned warehouse reduce columns/partitions scanned. I'd read the EXPLAIN plan to confirm the bottleneck (scan vs join vs sort).

ib-coding.q: Return the k most frequent elements in an array.
ib-coding.a: Count with a hash map, then take the k largest by count: return [x for x,_ in Counter(nums).most_common(k)] — O(n log k) with a size-k heap under the hood. State complexity and handle k>distinct and ties.

Sort by start; iterate, and if the current start <= last end, extend the last interval's end to max(last_end, cur_end), else append a new interval. O(n log n) for the sort. Edge cases: empty input, single interval, touching intervals.

ib-coding.q: How would you find the top 10 products by sales from a 1 TB file on a laptop?
ib-coding.a: Don't load it. Stream/chunk the file (or query Parquet with DuckDB/Polars), accumulate per-product totals in a dict, and keep the top 10 with heapq.nlargest — O(n) time, O(distinct products) memory. I'd call out memory and idempotency explicitly; the DuckDB GROUP BY ... ORDER BY ... LIMIT 10 over Parquet is the production answer.

Two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half, kept balanced. add() pushes/pops across them; median() is the top of the larger heap, or the average of both tops when equal sizes. O(log n) per insert, O(1) query.

ib-data-modeling.q: Model the data for a feed-engagement product (likes/comments/shares).
ib-data-modeling.a: Clarify questions (DAU, engagement per post, per cohort). Grain = one engagement event. Fact fct_engagement(user_sk, post_sk, date_sk, device_sk, event_type, count=1). Dims: dim_user (SCD2 on attributes), dim_post, dim_date, dim_device. Additive counts; build cohort/retention rollups in Gold. I'd name the grain first and justify star for fast BI aggregation.

ib-data-modeling.q: You need to report a customer's tier at the time of each purchase. How do you model the customer dimension?
ib-data-modeling.a: SCD Type 2: version dim_customer with valid_from/valid_to/is_current and a surrogate key. On a tier change, close the current row (set valid_to, is_current=false) and insert a new versioned row. The fact stores the surrogate key valid at purchase time, so point-in-time tier is correct — Type 1 would overwrite and lose history.

ib-data-modeling.q: How do you handle late-arriving data in a partitioned fact table?
ib-data-modeling.a: Partition by event date (not load date). On late arrival, reprocess/backfill the affected event-date partition idempotently (partition overwrite or MERGE on keys) rather than appending to today's partition. For late-arriving dimensions, insert an inferred/placeholder member and update its attributes when the full record arrives, so facts aren't orphaned.

ib-big-data.q: What is a shuffle in Spark and why is it expensive?
ib-big-data.a: A shuffle redistributes data by key across executors so related rows co-locate for a wide operation (join/groupBy/distinct). It serializes data, spills to disk, and moves it over the network, creating a stage boundary — the dominant cost. Reduce it by filtering/aggregating early, broadcasting small joins, and choosing good partition keys.

ib-big-data.q: One key holds 90% of the rows and a task straggles. Diagnose and fix.
ib-big-data.a: That's data skew — work concentrates on one partition/task. Fixes: salt the hot key (add a random suffix, aggregate in two passes), enable AQE skew-join handling (spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled), or broadcast the other side if it's small. Confirm via the Spark UI (one task far larger/slower).

ib-big-data.q: repartition vs coalesce — when do you use each?
ib-big-data.a: repartition(n) does a full shuffle to increase or rebalance partitions (e.g. before a heavy wide stage or to break skew). coalesce(n) reduces partitions without a full shuffle (e.g. to write fewer output files). coalesce can't increase partition count; repartition can.

ib-system-design.q: Design an analytics data warehouse for an e-commerce company.
ib-system-design.a: Clarify scale/SLAs/consumers. Ingest via Kafka Connect → Bronze (raw on object storage) → Silver (dbt clean/dedupe/conform, SCD2 dims) → Gold (pre-aggregated marts) on Snowflake/Databricks with separate compute for ETL vs BI. Star schema, partition by event date, idempotent loads, dbt freshness/volume tests + CI. Trade-offs: batch latency vs cost, normalize vs denormalize, build vs buy ingestion; wrap with monitoring and lineage.

ib-system-design.q: Design a real-time fraud detection pipeline.
ib-system-design.a: Transaction events → Kafka (many partitions) → Flink stateful processing (feature computation + rules/model, event-time with watermarks for late/out-of-order events) → alerts topic → action service; also sink to Iceberg for training/replay. Use exactly-once (checkpointing) where double-acting is costly. Trade-offs: exactly-once vs latency, state/window size vs memory, in-stream model vs feature-store lookup, alert precision vs recall.

ib-system-design.q: Design a pipeline that processes 10,000 documents/day through an LLM with rate limits, retries, and a cost budget.
ib-system-design.a: Queue + worker pool doing parse → chunk → embed → upsert to a vector store keyed by chunk-id (+ metadata/ACL). Operationally: exponential backoff/retry on rate limits, idempotency by doc-id, batch and cache embeddings, a cost-budget guard that throttles/halts when spend exceeds budget, and a dead-letter queue for poison docs. Trade-offs: throughput vs cost vs freshness; hosted vs self-hosted embeddings; rerank latency vs token savings. This is the RAG-ingestion pattern.

ib-behavioral.q: Tell me about a time you took ownership of a failure. (Ownership / Deliver Results)
ib-behavioral.a: STAR: a nightly pipeline failed silently and finance got stale numbers (S); I owned restoring trust and preventing recurrence (T); I added idempotent reruns, freshness/volume checks with alerting, and a backfill runbook, and ran a blameless postmortem (A); incidents dropped ~80% and sign-off moved 3h earlier (R). Lesson: observability is part of the deliverable. Keep it ~2 min, 'I' not 'we', quantified.

ib-behavioral.q: Describe a time you acted with integrity under pressure. (Values)
ib-behavioral.a: Pick a story showing you did the right thing for the client/firm even when inconvenient — e.g. flagging a data quality issue that delayed a release rather than shipping numbers you didn't trust, communicating transparently with stakeholders, and fixing the root cause. Frame around Goldman's values (Integrity, Client Service, Partnership) and quantify the avoided impact.

ib-behavioral.q: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager or team.
ib-behavioral.a: Use Disagree & Commit: state the decision, your evidence-based concern, how you prototyped/measured to make the case, and that you committed fully once the team decided — then the outcome. Emphasize 'I' actions and a quantified result, and show you can argue with data yet align and execute.

ib-company-playbooks.q: What does Goldman's data engineer technical screen typically include?
ib-company-playbooks.a: A CoderPad round (arrays, trees, recursion, simple graph traversal — judged on clarity, structure, correctness), medium-to-hard SQL (multi-join, window functions, CTEs, optimization), distributed-systems questions (Spark/Hadoop: shuffles, skew, joins), and data modeling / ETL architecture. Behavioral is values-based (Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence).

ib-company-playbooks.q: How is Meta's DE loop structured?
ib-company-playbooks.a: Recruiter → technical screen → onsite of four technical rounds plus a behavioral, often in one day. The technical rounds move through product sense, data modeling, SQL and Python, with data modeling weighted most and typically introduced through a product-sense prompt. Emphasize what you personally did and measurable impact.

ib-company-playbooks.q: What should I expect for a JPMorgan data engineer interview?
ib-company-playbooks.a: Recruiter screen → a technical interview (often Python; core CS, sometimes Java/concurrency and big-data concepts) → 4-5 shorter onsite rounds (~30-35 min) covering medium DSA in Python, SQL (joins, window functions, aggregations, subqueries), a case study, and behavioral including 'why JPMorgan/finance'. Breadth and clear motivation matter.

agentic-what.q: What is an AI agent, and how is it different from a normal LLM call?
agentic-what.a: An agent wraps an LLM in a loop so it can act, not just answer: reason → act (call a tool) → observe the result → repeat until the goal is met (the ReAct pattern). A plain LLM call is one-shot (prompt in, text out); an agent decides steps at runtime and uses tools (query a DB, call an API, run code). The trade-off is that autonomy needs guardrails because an agent can act confidently wrong.

agentic-what.q: When would you use an agent versus a fixed workflow for a data task?
agentic-what.a: Use a fixed workflow when the steps are known and deterministic — it's predictable and cheap. Use an agent when the task is open-ended and benefits from runtime decisions: investigating a pipeline failure, writing/validating SQL from a question, or diagnosing and repairing data issues. Agents are more flexible but require bounds (allowed tools, validation, HITL) since they choose their own steps and can err.

agentic-mcp.q: Explain MCP and why it matters for a data platform.
agentic-mcp.a: MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard for connecting agents to tools and data — created by Anthropic, now under the Linux Foundation, backed by OpenAI/Google/Microsoft/AWS. It's client-server over JSON-RPC with three primitives: Tools (callable functions), Resources (readable data), Prompts (templates). For a data platform you wrap a source once as an MCP server (e.g. a read-only run_sql tool + schema resources) and every MCP-aware agent can use it with the auth/scoping you control — ending N×M bespoke integrations.

agentic-mcp.q: How would you safely expose your production warehouse to agents via MCP?
agentic-mcp.a: Build a warehouse MCP server with least-privilege, read-only tools by default (run_sql restricted to SELECT, parameterized), schema resources for grounding, and gate any writes behind explicit approval. Enforce per-team auth, row/column scoping, query cost/byte limits, input validation, and audit logging of every call. Treat the MCP server as a production data interface — it is one — and add monitoring + rate limits so an agent loop can't exfiltrate data or run up cost.

agentic-a2a.q: What's the difference between MCP and A2A, and when do you use each?
agentic-a2a.a: MCP is vertical — it connects a single agent to tools and data (Tools/Resources/Prompts). A2A (Agent2Agent) is horizontal — it lets agents delegate subtasks to each other, advertising capabilities via Agent Cards over HTTP/JSON. They're complementary: an orchestrator delegates work via A2A while each specialist does the actual work via MCP tools. Use A2A when you need a team of specialized agents; a single tool-rich agent (MCP only) is enough for simpler tasks.

agentic-frameworks.q: How do you choose an agent framework for a production data system?
agentic-frameworks.a: Start from your dominant constraint. Need fine control, retries, and human-in-the-loop over a stateful workflow → LangGraph (state-machine model). All-in on Anthropic/MCP → Claude Agent SDK. Fast role-based multi-agent prototype → CrewAI. Conversational/research multi-agent → AutoGen/AG2. RAG-grounded over your data → LlamaIndex. Strict typing → Pydantic AI. Don't pick by hype; real systems often combine frameworks (e.g. CrewAI orchestration + LangGraph for the critical controlled path).

agentic-pipelines.q: Design a self-healing data pipeline using agentic AI.
agentic-pipelines.a: An orchestrator coordinates detect/diagnose/repair specialist agents over the pipeline. Detect: monitor for null floods, schema drift, freshness misses, volume anomalies. Diagnose: the agent reads logs + lineage to find root cause. Act: auto-fix safe, known cases (retry, backfill the affected partition, re-cast a changed type, quarantine bad rows) and escalate ambiguous/risky changes to a human (HITL) with the diagnosis attached. Define an intent layer (purpose + freshness/accuracy/reliability) so the agent knows what 'healthy' means, and bound everything with guardrails/validation — safe action, not detection, is the hard part. Specialists touch data via MCP.

agentic-text-to-sql.q: How do you stop a text-to-SQL agent from returning wrong or dangerous queries?
agentic-text-to-sql.a: Ground generation in the real schema/catalog, then gate execution behind validation: dry-run/EXPLAIN for validity, check bytes-scanned/cost against a budget, run tests/expected-shape checks, and confirm it answers the question. Execute only after it passes, return the result plus the SQL for human audit, and require approval for writes or expensive jobs. The key principle: generated SQL can run yet be business-wrong (wrong join grain, missing filter), and confidently-wrong output corrupts everything downstream — so never auto-trust it. Tools like Databricks Genie Code and Snowflake Cortex Code lifted success precisely by adding planning + validation.

agentic-governance.q: What guardrails would you put around autonomous agents acting on production data?
agentic-governance.a: Least-privilege permissions (read-only by default, writes gated), pre-execution validation (dry-run, schema/contract checks, cost/bytes limits), cost and rate limits so a loop can't run up spend, and human-in-the-loop approval for risky/irreversible actions (agent proposes, human disposes). Plus treat agents like software: eval suites with known-good outcomes, success/accuracy metrics, and production monitoring/tracing of every action. The reason is that agents can be confidently wrong and that error propagates through every downstream system — pipelines feeding AI need an even higher bar.

agentic-governance.q: How does the data engineer's role change in an agentic world?
agentic-governance.a: From executing every step to orchestrating agent fleets: defining policies and guardrails, designing how agents collaborate, building the validation/eval layer, and owning the edge cases where human judgment matters. You remain accountable for what the fleet does — who can approve writes, what's logged, how incidents are handled. The skill shifts from writing each transformation to designing safe, observable, well-bounded autonomous systems.

agentic-anatomy.q: What are the components of an AI agent?
agentic-anatomy.a: Four parts: the LLM (reasoning core that decides the next step), tools (functions it calls via function calling — typed name/description/args), memory (short-term context window + long-term vector store for recall), and planning (decompose the goal; ReAct does it per-step, advanced agents plan explicitly and reflect). Put together: agent = LLM + tools + memory + planning, run in a loop. Frameworks just structure these four differently.

agentic-anatomy.q: How does an agent actually call a tool, and what makes a tool well-designed?
agentic-anatomy.a: Via function calling: each tool has a name, description, and typed (JSON-schema) signature; the LLM emits a structured call like {"tool":"run_sql","query":"..."}, the runtime executes it, and the result is fed back into the agent's context. Well-designed tools are the biggest lever on reliability: clear names/descriptions (the model selects tools by description), narrow scope, validated inputs, and least-privilege access.

agentic-multi-agent.q: Why use multiple specialized agents instead of one large agent, and what does it cost?
agentic-multi-agent.a: Narrow specialists (ingestion, transform, quality, repair) keep each agent's context focused, which means better prompts, fewer mistakes, and the ability to evaluate and guardrail each agent independently — plus reuse and parallelism. An orchestrator decomposes the goal and delegates (via A2A) while specialists act via MCP tools. The cost is more moving parts, so you must invest in observability and tracing to see who did what and debug/attribute cost across the fleet.

agentic-data-quality.q: How would an agent improve data quality beyond static dbt/Great Expectations tests?
agentic-data-quality.a: It augments those tests rather than replacing them. A data-quality agent runs the checks (ranges, nulls, uniqueness, referential integrity, contract/schema, SLAs), but adds judgment: it investigates a failure by reading lineage and sampling bad rows, hypothesizes a root cause, adapts thresholds to seasonality, quarantines bad records to a dead-letter store, and routes the issue (alert/PR/hand to a repair agent) with a plain-language explanation. Because it can be wrong, it needs its own evals and human review for consequential calls, with humans setting the policies and contracts it enforces.

bd-evo-overview.q: Walk me through the evolution of big data technologies.
bd-evo-overview.a: Four eras, each fixing the last's wall. Pre-2003: RDBMS/warehouses scaled UP on one server and hit a cost/size wall. 2003-2012 Hadoop era: Google's GFS (2003) + MapReduce (2004) papers → Hadoop (2006) scaled OUT across commodity clusters with HDFS + MapReduce (batch, disk-heavy). 2009-2016: Spark brought in-memory speed, NoSQL (Cassandra/Mongo) scaled flexible data, Kafka+Flink added real-time streaming. 2014-2026: cloud separated storage from compute (Snowflake/BigQuery), and table formats (Hudi/Delta/Iceberg) created the lakehouse. Today Iceberg is the standard, real-time OLAP is mainstream, and AI/agentic is the next wave.

bd-evo-overview.q: Why did the industry move from scaling up to scaling out?
bd-evo-overview.a: Scaling up (a bigger single server) gets exponentially expensive and has a hard ceiling — eventually data won't fit or process on one machine. Google's GFS/MapReduce papers showed you could instead spread storage and compute across many cheap commodity machines (scale out), tolerating failures via replication and task re-runs. Hadoop made that open source, enabling petabyte-scale, fault-tolerant, cost-effective processing — the foundation of big data.

bd-evo-hadoop.q: Explain HDFS and MapReduce and how they work together.
bd-evo-hadoop.a: HDFS splits a large file into big blocks (e.g. 128MB), replicates them (default 3×) across DataNodes with a NameNode tracking metadata — cheap, fault-tolerant, petabyte-scale storage. MapReduce is the batch compute model: map processes each block/split in parallel emitting (key,value), shuffle groups values by key across the cluster, reduce aggregates per key. Crucially it moves compute to the data (runs maps where blocks live) and tolerates failures by re-running tasks. YARN (Hadoop 2.0) later separated resource management so engines like Spark could share the cluster.

bd-evo-hadoop.q: Why did MapReduce fall out of favor, and what replaced it?
bd-evo-hadoop.a: MapReduce writes intermediate results to disk between every stage, making it slow — especially for iterative (ML) and interactive workloads — and it's verbose to write. Spark replaced it for compute by keeping data in memory with a DAG execution model (10-100× faster) and far easier DataFrame/SQL APIs. Meanwhile the cloud + object storage removed HDFS's operational burden. The legacy survives though: the Hive metastore became modern catalogs, HDFS ideas informed object storage, and Spark inherited the map/shuffle/reduce model.

bd-evo-spark-streaming.q: Why is Spark faster than Hadoop MapReduce?
bd-evo-spark-streaming.a: Spark keeps intermediate data in memory and builds a DAG of stages with lazy evaluation, so it avoids MapReduce's disk writes between every stage and can pipeline work. That makes it 10-100× faster, especially for iterative algorithms (ML) and interactive queries. It also unifies batch, SQL, streaming, and ML in one engine with high-level DataFrame/SQL APIs, versus MapReduce's low-level, disk-based, two-stage model.

bd-evo-spark-streaming.q: Contrast NoSQL with relational databases and say when you'd choose each.
bd-evo-spark-streaming.a: Relational DBs give strict schemas, ACID, and rich joins — ideal for transactional, well-structured data needing consistency. NoSQL (inspired by Amazon's Dynamo paper; Cassandra, MongoDB) trades those for horizontal scale, flexible/schema-light models (document, wide-column, key-value), high write throughput, and availability (often AP in CAP). Choose NoSQL for web-scale, high-velocity, semi-structured workloads with simple access patterns; choose relational for complex queries, joins, and strong consistency. Many systems use both (polyglot persistence).

bd-evo-lakehouse-today.q: What is the lakehouse and what problem did it solve?
bd-evo-lakehouse-today.a: A data lake (raw files on cheap object storage) is economical but chaotic — no transactions, no schema enforcement, no updates/undo, so a failed or concurrent write corrupts data. Table formats (Apache Iceberg, Delta Lake, Apache Hudi) add a metadata/transaction layer over Parquet giving ACID, time travel, and schema evolution. That delivers warehouse-grade reliability on data-lake economics — the lakehouse. Combined with cloud separation of storage and compute and a REST catalog, you get one cheap copy of data queryable by many engines.

bd-evo-lakehouse-today.q: Why is separating storage and compute such a big deal in the cloud era?
bd-evo-lakehouse-today.a: On-prem Hadoop coupled storage and compute on fixed hardware, so you over-provisioned and couldn't scale them independently. Cloud platforms (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) put data cheaply on object storage and spin compute up/down on demand. That enables elasticity (size compute to the query), workload isolation (separate compute for BI vs ETL over one copy of data), pay-per-use, and serverless — the economic foundation of the modern lakehouse.

cloud-server-vs-serverless.q: When would you choose serverless over provisioned servers, and when not?
cloud-server-vs-serverless.a: Choose serverless (Lambda/Cloud Run/BigQuery/Athena) for bursty, unpredictable, event-driven, or low-frequency work — you get zero ops, auto-scaling to zero, and pay-per-use. Avoid it for sustained high-concurrency (~24/7) workloads (a right-sized container/cluster is far cheaper), for low-latency always-on services (cold starts hurt), for long-running or special-runtime jobs (timeouts/limits), and where you need full control. The gold-standard answer is hybrid: steady core on servers/containers, bursts on serverless — often ~60% cheaper than all-serverless.

cloud-server-vs-serverless.q: Why might a bank run its own Spark clusters instead of going fully serverless?
cloud-server-vs-serverless.a: Banks have steady, predictable, regulated workloads where provisioned compute is cheaper at constant high utilization and gives full control over runtime, libraries, networking, and data residency — important for compliance and long-running jobs that would hit serverless timeouts. They still use serverless for bursty glue and event-driven ingestion. It's a hybrid: control/cost/compliance from servers, elasticity from serverless where it fits.

model-mpp-greenplum.q: Explain Greenplum's MPP architecture and the role of the distribution key.
model-mpp-greenplum.a: Greenplum is a shared-nothing MPP database: a coordinator (master) parses, plans, dispatches and gathers (holding no user data), while segments — each with its own CPU/memory/disk — store a distinct slice of every table and process queries in parallel, shuffling over the interconnect for joins. The distribution key (DISTRIBUTED BY) is the #1 performance lever: a good high-cardinality, evenly-spread key (ideally the join key) spreads work evenly and keeps joins local; a bad one causes data skew (one segment does all the work) or expensive redistribution.

model-mpp-greenplum.q: You're migrating Greenplum to a cloud warehouse. What transfers and what changes?
model-mpp-greenplum.a: Concepts transfer directly: the distribution key maps to clustering/partitioning, segments map to elastic cloud compute, and append-optimized columnar+compression maps to the same columnar storage. The big change is that Greenplum couples storage and compute on fixed hardware, while cloud warehouses (Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks) separate them — giving elasticity, workload isolation, pay-per-use, and far less ops. SQL is largely portable (Greenplum is Postgres-derived); you re-tune distribution→clustering and right-size compute instead of cluster nodes.

pipe-enterprise-etl.q: How does DataStage achieve parallelism, and how would you modernize a DataStage job?
pipe-enterprise-etl.a: DataStage uses two parallelisms: pipeline parallelism (stages stream rows concurrently, like an assembly line) and partition parallelism (the same job logic runs on data partitions across many nodes) — the same model as Spark. To modernize, map each stage to cloud-native code: source/target connectors → Spark readers/writers (or Auto Loader over GCS/S3), transformer/derivation → PySpark or dbt, lookup/join/aggregator → Spark/dbt SQL, and the job sequence → an orchestrator (Workflows/Airflow) plus CI/CD. Banks do this incrementally — keep trusted DataStage running while moving logic to versioned, testable code.

pipe-enterprise-etl.q: What's a common correctness bug in parallel ETL, and how do you avoid it?
pipe-enterprise-etl.a: Wrong partitioning: if data is partitioned so that related rows land on different nodes, per-partition operations like aggregation, dedup, or sequence generation produce incorrect results. Avoid it by partitioning on the key the operation groups by (e.g. partition by account_id before aggregating per account), and validate with reconciliation/row-count and uniqueness tests. It's the same discipline as choosing a Spark repartition key or a Greenplum distribution key.

cap-bank-platform.q: Design an end-to-end data platform for a bank handling both batch and streaming.
cap-bank-platform.a: Use a Lambda architecture over a governed lakehouse. Batch: source extracts → DataStage (or Spark) ETL standardizes/validates and lands bronze on GCS → PySpark/Databricks builds silver (dedupe, type, SCD2 dimensions, referential-integrity quality) → load Greenplum/cloud warehouse → dbt builds gold marts (reconciliation control totals, regulatory exposure) with tests. Speed: card transactions → Kafka → Spark Structured Streaming scores fraud/AML in real time → alerts. Cross-cutting: data contracts enforced as a CI gate, lineage and access via a catalog, automated reconciliation, and CI/CD so nothing ships red. The result is a reconciled golden source with controls and audit trail — what regulators expect. Reconcile the speed and batch layers so real-time and EOD numbers agree.

cap-bank-platform.q: How do you ensure data quality and regulatory trust in a bank pipeline?
cap-bank-platform.a: Layered controls: reject invalid rows at ingestion (DataStage reject link), dedupe and enforce referential integrity in silver (PySpark), enforce data contracts (schema/SLA) as a CI gate that blocks bad data before consumers, run dbt tests on gold marts (unique/not_null/accepted_values), and produce reconciliation control totals reconciled against source systems with breaks investigated within SLA. Add lineage (catalog), audit trails, and CI/CD so every change is tested and traceable — giving a golden source that's trustworthy internally and under regulatory inspection.

model-bi-serving.q: What is a semantic (metrics) layer and why use one?
model-bi-serving.a: A governed layer that defines each business metric once — its measure, dimensions, and the grain/joins it's valid at — so every consumer (Power BI, Looker, Tableau, notebooks, APIs) computes it identically. It exists to kill metric drift: without it, teams re-implement 'revenue' or 'active customers' differently and dashboards disagree. Implementations include dbt MetricFlow/semantic models, LookML, Cube, AtScale, or governed SQL metric views. Consumers query metrics, not raw tables, so the number is consistent from pipeline to boardroom.

model-bi-serving.q: How does the serving layer fit after gold marts, and why does it matter in a bank?
model-bi-serving.a: Silver gives clean data, gold gives business marts, and the serving layer exposes governed metrics over those marts to BI tools, extracts, and APIs (with caching/aggregates for speed). In a bank it's critical because executives and regulators must get one trusted, reconciled number; conflicting figures across reports are a compliance risk. So you define metrics once over the golden source and let every tool read that definition rather than re-deriving it.

model-galaxy-schema.q: What is a galaxy (fact constellation) schema and when do you use it?
model-galaxy-schema.a: A galaxy/fact-constellation schema has multiple fact tables (e.g. sales, returns, shipments) that share the same conformed dimensions. You use it whenever a warehouse models more than one business process — which is almost always. Each process gets its own fact at its own grain, and they reuse one set of conformed dimensions (dim_date, dim_product, dim_customer) so you can analyze across processes (e.g. returns as a % of sales by product). It's essentially multiple stars sharing dimensions.

model-galaxy-schema.q: Why are conformed dimensions critical in a galaxy schema?
model-galaxy-schema.a: Because cross-process analysis is only valid if both facts mean the same thing by 'product' or 'date'. A conformed dimension is the identical dimension (same keys, attributes, meaning) reused across facts. If each star had its own slightly different dim_product, you couldn't reliably join sales and returns. Conformed dimensions are the backbone of Kimball's bus architecture and what makes a galaxy schema an integrated whole rather than disconnected stars.

model-schema-types.q: Compare star, snowflake, galaxy, and One Big Table schemas.
model-schema-types.a: They sit on the normalize↔denormalize spectrum. Star: a fact plus denormalized dimensions — few joins, fast, the default for one process. Snowflake: a star with normalized dimensions split into sub-tables — less redundancy, more joins; only for big/shared hierarchies. Galaxy/fact-constellation: multiple facts sharing conformed dimensions — the real enterprise shape. One Big Table: fully denormalized single wide table, zero joins — fastest on columnar warehouses for a specific dashboard, less reusable. In practice you use them together: star per process, conform into a galaxy, snowflake or OBT where it pays.

model-schema-types.q: You're modeling sales, returns, and shipments together — which schema?
model-schema-types.a: A galaxy (fact constellation): three fact tables (one per process, each at its own grain) sharing conformed dimensions. You wouldn't force them into one fact; you model each as a star and conform the shared dimensions so they integrate.

dbms-what.q: What does a DBMS give you over storing data in files?
dbms-what.a: A DBMS provides a single shared store (no duplication), integrity constraints (bad/orphaned data rejected), transactions and concurrency control (many users safely at once), a query language (SQL) for declarative access, plus security, backup, and crash recovery. Files have none of these — they duplicate data, can't enforce integrity, corrupt under concurrent writes, and lose data on a crash.

dbms-what.q: Is a data warehouse a DBMS? Why?
dbms-what.a: Yes — it stores, manages, and serves shared data with querying, integrity, and concurrency, which are the defining DBMS responsibilities. Warehouses, lakehouses, and even NoSQL stores are all kinds of DBMS; they differ in data model and trade-offs, not in being database management systems.

dbms-types.q: Walk through the main types of DBMS and where each fits.
dbms-types.a: Relational (tables + SQL + ACID + joins) for structured data, OLTP, BI. Document (JSON, MongoDB) for semi-structured app data. Key-value (Redis, DynamoDB) for fast lookups/caching. Column-family (Cassandra) for high write scale. Graph (Neo4j) for relationship traversal. Hierarchical/network are legacy. NewSQL (CockroachDB, Spanner) gives SQL+ACID at horizontal scale. Time-series/vector for metrics and AI embeddings. The big split is relational (SQL) vs NoSQL; choose by data shape and access pattern.

dbms-types.q: Relational vs NoSQL in one line each.
dbms-types.a: Relational: tables with SQL, ACID, and joins — best for structured data needing integrity. NoSQL: flexible/schema-light models (document, key-value, column, graph) that scale out horizontally, often with eventual consistency — best for scale, flexibility, or a specialized shape.

dbms-architecture.q: Describe the main components of a DBMS and how a query flows.
dbms-architecture.a: A query processor (parser → optimizer → executor), a storage engine (access methods like B-trees + a buffer pool caching hot pages), a transaction manager (ACID, concurrency via locks/MVCC), a recovery/log manager (WAL), and a catalog (metadata). A query flows: client SQL → parser (validate, resolve names via catalog) → optimizer (pick cheapest plan from statistics + indexes) → executor → storage engine over the buffer pool — all under the transaction manager with the WAL ensuring durability.

dbms-architecture.q: Which two components explain most performance and reliability?
dbms-architecture.a: Performance: the optimizer (give it good statistics and the right indexes so it picks fast plans) and the buffer pool (a high cache-hit rate keeps work in memory). Reliability: the transaction manager plus the write-ahead log, which logs every change before data so a crash recovers to a consistent state.

dbms-three-schema.q: Explain the three-schema architecture and data independence.
dbms-three-schema.a: Three levels: external (per-user views/subsets), conceptual (the full logical schema — tables, relationships, constraints), and internal (physical storage — files, pages, indexes). Separating them gives data independence: logical (change the conceptual schema without breaking apps/views) and physical (change storage like adding an index without changing the logical schema). It's why you can tune storage or refactor with zero app changes.

dbms-three-schema.q: Adding an index without touching any application relies on what?
dbms-three-schema.a: Physical data independence — changing the internal/physical level (storage, indexes, layout) without affecting the conceptual schema or the applications above it.

dbms-transactions-acid.a: Atomicity: a transaction's steps all commit or all roll back (no partial work). Consistency: it moves the database from one valid state to another, respecting constraints. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other (as if serial). Durability: once committed, changes survive crashes. Atomicity/durability come from the write-ahead log; isolation from locks or MVCC.

dbms-transactions-acid.q: ACID vs BASE — when would you accept BASE?
dbms-transactions-acid.a: ACID guarantees strict correctness (atomic, consistent, isolated, durable) — essential for systems of record like ledgers. BASE (basically available, soft state, eventual consistency) relaxes consistency/isolation for availability and horizontal scale, used by many NoSQL stores. You accept BASE when massive scale and availability matter more than immediate strict consistency (e.g. a product catalog or social feed), not for money movement.

dbms-concurrency.q: Explain the read anomalies and how isolation levels address them.
dbms-concurrency.a: Dirty read: seeing another transaction's uncommitted change. Non-repeatable read: a row's value changes between two reads in your transaction. Phantom read: a range query returns new rows on re-execution. Isolation levels prevent progressively more: Read Uncommitted (none), Read Committed (no dirty), Repeatable Read (no dirty/non-repeatable), Serializable (none of the three). Stricter levels reduce concurrency, so pick the lowest level that's still correct.

Locking (two-phase locking) takes shared/exclusive locks to serialize conflicting access — simple but causes contention and possible deadlocks (the DBMS aborts a victim). MVCC keeps multiple row versions so readers see a consistent snapshot as of their start, meaning readers never block writers and vice versa (Postgres, Oracle, InnoDB) — generally far better concurrency, at the cost of cleaning up old versions (VACUUM).

dbms-recovery.q: How does write-ahead logging guarantee durability and atomicity?
dbms-recovery.a: The WAL rule writes each change to a durable, sequential log before the data pages, and flushes the log on commit. So a committed change is always in the log (durability). On crash, recovery replays the log: REDO committed transactions whose data may not have hit disk, and UNDO uncommitted ones (atomicity). Checkpoints bound how far back recovery must replay.

dbms-recovery.q: What are REDO and UNDO in crash recovery?
dbms-recovery.a: REDO re-applies the changes of committed transactions that may not have reached the data files yet; UNDO rolls back the changes of transactions that hadn't committed at crash time. Together they restore a consistent state.

dbms-storage-indexing.q: Why is a B-tree the default index, and when would a hash index be better?
dbms-storage-indexing.a: A B-tree keeps keys sorted, so it supports equality, range queries, and ordered scans in O(log n) — covering most query patterns. A hash index gives O(1) equality lookups but can't do ranges or ordering, so it's only better for pure equality lookups. Most general-purpose indexes are B-trees.

dbms-storage-indexing.q: What's the trade-off with adding indexes, and how do you decide what to index?
dbms-storage-indexing.a: Indexes make reads fast but slow writes (each insert/update maintains them) and consume storage. Index the columns you frequently filter, join, or sort on — not everything. A covering index (containing all columns a query needs) is ideal because the query is answered from the index without touching the table.

dbms-query-processing.q: Why can the same SQL query be fast one day and slow another?
dbms-query-processing.a: SQL is declarative; the optimizer chooses an execution plan (join order, join algorithm, index vs scan) using cost estimates from table statistics. If statistics go stale or an index is missing/dropped, the optimizer can pick a worse plan — turning a fast indexed lookup into a slow full scan. You investigate with EXPLAIN and fix by refreshing statistics and adding the right indexes.

dbms-query-processing.q: What are the optimizer's main decisions?
dbms-query-processing.a: Access path (index seek vs full table scan), join algorithm (nested-loop for small/indexed, hash for large unsorted equality, merge for sorted inputs), and join order (filter and aggregate early to shrink intermediate results). It estimates costs from statistics to choose.

dbms-security-admin.q: How do you implement least-privilege access in a database?
dbms-security-admin.a: Use role-based access control: create roles (e.g. analyst, app_rw) granted only the specific privileges they need (SELECT on certain schemas, etc.), then assign roles to users with GRANT — rather than granting privileges per user. Combine with authentication (SSO/certs), encryption at rest and in transit, row/column-level security and masking for PII, and auditing. This minimizes blast radius and is what regulators expect.

dbms-security-admin.q: What are the core responsibilities of a DBA?
dbms-security-admin.a: Backups and tested restores, recovery/HA planning, performance tuning (indexes, statistics, slow queries), capacity planning, upgrades/patching, replication/high availability, and user/security management — keeping the database healthy, available, and secure. On managed cloud DBs these shift but don't disappear.

rdbms-relational-model.q: Explain the relational model's core terms.
rdbms-relational-model.a: A relation is a table; a tuple is a row; an attribute is a column; a domain is the set of allowed values (type) for an attribute. Degree is the number of columns, cardinality the number of rows. Rules: each cell holds one atomic value (1NF), rows are unordered and unique (a set), and columns are referenced by name. Because relations are sets, SQL and relational algebra operate on them cleanly.

rdbms-relational-model.q: Why does the relational model being set-based matter?
rdbms-relational-model.a: Because a relation is a set of tuples, queries are operations on sets (select, project, join, union), which are mathematically well-defined and freely reorderable. That lets the optimizer rewrite a query into an equivalent cheaper plan, and lets SQL be declarative — you say what you want and the engine decides how. It's the foundation of relational databases' predictability and power.

rdbms-keys.q: Differentiate super, candidate, and primary keys, plus foreign keys.
rdbms-keys.a: A super key is any column set that uniquely identifies a row (possibly with extra columns). A candidate key is a minimal super key (no removable column). The primary key is the chosen candidate — unique and NOT NULL (entity integrity). A composite key spans 2+ columns. A foreign key references another table's primary key, enforcing referential integrity (must match an existing PK or be NULL). Nesting: super ⊇ candidate ⊇ primary.

rdbms-keys.q: Natural vs surrogate keys — which and when?
rdbms-keys.a: A natural key is real-world data (email, SSN, SKU); a surrogate key is a meaningless generated integer. Warehouses favor surrogates because they enable SCD2 history (one business entity has many versioned rows) and give small, stable, fast join keys. OLTP can use either. You typically keep both — the surrogate as the PK that facts join on, the natural key as a lookup attribute.

rdbms-integrity.q: Name the integrity constraints and why enforce them in the database.
rdbms-integrity.a: Entity integrity (PK unique and NOT NULL — every row identifiable), referential integrity (FK matches an existing PK or is NULL — no orphans), and domain integrity (values fit the column's type/range/rules via data types, CHECK, UNIQUE, NOT NULL). Enforcing them in the DBMS means invalid data is rejected automatically even if application code has a bug — a single reliable guardian of correctness, which is exactly what regulated systems require.

rdbms-integrity.q: What does ON DELETE CASCADE do?
rdbms-integrity.a: When a referenced primary-key row is deleted, its child rows (those whose foreign key points to it) are automatically deleted too — keeping referential integrity. Alternatives are RESTRICT/NO ACTION (block the delete if children exist) and SET NULL (null out the children's FK).

rdbms-relational-algebra.q: What is relational algebra and how does it relate to SQL?
rdbms-relational-algebra.a: Relational algebra is the formal set of operations on relations: σ select (filter rows → WHERE), π project (pick columns → SELECT), ⋈ join (combine → JOIN), × Cartesian product (CROSS JOIN), ∪ ∩ − set operations, and ρ rename (AS). SQL is a declarative language over this algebra — every query maps to an operator tree that the optimizer rewrites into an equivalent, cheaper one (e.g. pushing selection below a join).

rdbms-relational-algebra.q: What does 'push selection below the join' mean and why does it help?
rdbms-relational-algebra.a: It means apply the filter (σ) before the join (⋈) rather than after. Filtering first shrinks the inputs to the join, so the join processes far fewer rows — much less work. It's a standard, algebra-justified optimizer rewrite, and it's why putting selective predicates so they reduce data before a big join matters for performance.

rdbms-er-modeling.q: How do you map an ER diagram to relational tables?
rdbms-er-modeling.a: Each entity becomes a table with its key attribute as the primary key; attributes become columns. A 1:N relationship becomes a foreign key on the 'many' side. An M:N relationship becomes a junction/bridge table holding foreign keys to both entities (often with the pair as a composite key). 1:1 can use a FK on either side or be merged. Multi-valued attributes get their own table. ER modeling captures the business logic first, then the mapping is mechanical.

rdbms-er-modeling.q: How do you model a many-to-many relationship (students ↔ courses)?
rdbms-er-modeling.a: Create a junction (bridge) table — e.g. enrollment(student_id FK → students, course_id FK → courses) — with the pair as a composite primary key. Each row represents one student enrolled in one course, and you can add attributes like enrolled_date to the junction.

rdbms-normalization.q: Explain normalization through 3NF with the anomalies it prevents.
rdbms-normalization.a: Normalization removes redundancy that causes update, insert, and delete anomalies. 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups. 2NF: 1NF plus no partial dependency (non-key columns depend on the whole composite key, not part). 3NF: 2NF plus no transitive dependency (non-key columns depend only on the key, not on another non-key). Mnemonic: every non-key column depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key. BCNF is a stricter 3NF where every determinant is a candidate key.

rdbms-normalization.q: When do you normalize vs denormalize?
rdbms-normalization.a: Normalize OLTP schemas (aim for 3NF/BCNF) for integrity and small, safe writes — no anomalies. Denormalize for analytics (star schemas, One Big Table) for read speed, since reads vastly outnumber writes and joins are the cost; the denormalized data is rebuilt from a normalized source so redundancy stays safe. Knowing both directions and when to apply each is the real skill.

rdbms-acid-internals.q: How does a real RDBMS implement ACID internally?
rdbms-acid-internals.a: Atomicity and durability come from the write-ahead log: changes are logged before data and the log is flushed on commit, so recovery REDOes committed and UNDOes uncommitted transactions after a crash. Isolation comes from MVCC (row versions + snapshots so readers don't block writers, as in Postgres/Oracle/InnoDB) or locking (two-phase locking), tuned by isolation levels. Consistency is upheld by constraints plus rolling back violating transactions. This is why Postgres needs VACUUM (MVCC leaves dead versions) and why long transactions hurt.

rdbms-acid-internals.q: Why do long-running transactions degrade a Postgres database?
rdbms-acid-internals.a: Under MVCC, an open transaction holds a snapshot, so the database can't VACUUM (reclaim) row versions that might still be visible to it. Dead tuples accumulate (bloat), indexes grow, and performance degrades. Long transactions also increase lock/contention risk. The fix is to keep transactions short and let VACUUM reclaim versions.

rdbms-indexes-joins.q: Explain the join algorithms and when each is chosen.
rdbms-indexes-joins.a: Nested-loop join: for each row of the outer table, look up matches in the inner — great when one side is small or indexed. Hash join: build a hash table on the smaller input and probe with the larger — best for large, unsorted, equality joins. Merge join: both inputs sorted on the key, then merged — best when data is already sorted or indexed on the key. The optimizer picks based on statistics, and these mirror Spark's broadcast (≈nested-loop) and sort-merge joins.

An index that contains all the columns a query needs (the filter, join, and selected columns), so the engine can answer the query entirely from the index without reading the table — an 'index-only scan'. It speeds up hot queries at the cost of a wider index.

rdbms-vs-nosql.q: When do you choose an RDBMS vs NoSQL?
rdbms-vs-nosql.a: Choose an RDBMS when data is structured with relationships and you need integrity, ACID transactions, and flexible joins/SQL — OLTP, BI, systems of record like a ledger. Choose NoSQL when you need massive horizontal scale, flexible/evolving schemas, or a specialized shape (graph relationships, key lookups, time-series), and can accept eventual consistency (BASE). NewSQL aims for SQL+ACID at scale. Many systems use both — polyglot persistence — an RDBMS of record plus a cache and search/graph store.

Using multiple datastore types together, each for its strength — for example an RDBMS as the system of record, Redis for caching, and a search or graph store for specific access patterns — rather than forcing one database to do everything.
